Traduire en termes probabilistes : $X=(X_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ est une variable aléatoire à valeurs dans $(\mathbb{R}^{\mathbb{N}},\mathcal{C})$ ssi chaque $X_n = f_n \circ X$ est une v.a. réelle, i.e. ssi $(X_n)_n$ est un processus stochastique.

front_en: **Exercise 1.** Check that $(X_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ is a (real-valued) stochastic process if and only if it is a random variable taking values in the product space $\mathbb{R}^{\mathbb{N}}$.
back_en:
**Solution.** By definition, the product $\sigma$-algebra on $\mathbb{R}^{\mathbb{N}}$, denoted $\mathcal{C}$, is the smallest $\sigma$-algebra on $\mathbb{R}^{\mathbb{N}}$ with respect to which the coordinate maps
$$\big(f_n : (x_k)_{k\in\mathbb{N}} \in \mathbb{R}^{\mathbb{N}} \mapsto x_n\big)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$$
are measurable.

Thus, a function $F : (\mathbb{X}, \mathcal{A}) \to (\mathbb{R}^{\mathbb{N}}, \mathcal{C})$ is measurable if and only if for each $n \in \mathbb{N}$, the function $f_n \circ F : (\mathbb{X}, \mathcal{A}) \to (\mathbb{R}, \mathcal{B}(\mathbb{R}))$ is measurable.

In probabilistic terms, $X = (X_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}} : (\Omega, \mathcal{F}) \to (\mathbb{R}^{\mathbb{N}}, \mathcal{C})$ is a random variable if and only if for each $n \in \mathbb{N}$, $X_n : (\Omega, \mathcal{F}) \to (\mathbb{R}, \mathcal{B}(\mathbb{R}))$ is a random variable. $\square$

points.label: Characterize the product $\sigma$-algebra $\mathcal{C}$ on $\mathbb{R}^{\mathbb{N}}$ as the smallest $\sigma$-algebra making the coordinate maps $f_n : (x_k)_{k\in\mathbb{N}} \mapsto x_n$ measurable.

Deduce the measurability criterion: $F : (\mathbb{X},\mathcal{A}) \to (\mathbb{R}^{\mathbb{N}}, \mathcal{C})$ is measurable if and only if each composition $f_n \circ F$ is measurable (universal property of the product $\sigma$-algebra).

Translate into probabilistic terms: $X=(X_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ is a random variable taking values in $(\mathbb{R}^{\mathbb{N}},\mathcal{C})$ iff each $X_n = f_n \circ X$ is a real-valued random variable, i.e. iff $(X_n)_n$ is a stochastic process.

front: **Exercice 2.** Soit $(X_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ un processus stochastique, et posons $\mathcal{F}_n = \sigma(X_0, \ldots, X_n)$ pour tout $n \in \mathbb{N}$. Vérifier que $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ est la plus petite filtration pour laquelle $(X_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ est adapté.
back:
**Solution.** Si $(\mathcal{G}_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ est une filtration par rapport à laquelle $(X_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ est adapté, alors pour chaque $n \in \mathbb{N}$, les variables aléatoires $X_0, \ldots, X_n$ sont $\mathcal{G}_n$-mesurables (en effet, $X_n$ est $\mathcal{G}_n$-mesurable, $X_{n-1}$ est $\mathcal{G}_{n-1}$-mesurable donc $\mathcal{G}_n$-mesurable, etc.). Ainsi, on doit avoir $\mathcal{G}_n \supset \sigma(X_0, \ldots, X_n)$.

Réciproquement, $(\mathcal{F}_n = \sigma(X_0, \ldots, X_n))_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ est une filtration par rapport à laquelle $(X_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ est adapté. $\square$

points.label: Minoration : pour toute filtration $(\mathcal{G}_n)$ rendant $(X_n)$ adapté, montrer que $X_0, \ldots, X_n$ sont toutes $\mathcal{G}_n$-mesurables (via l'inclusion croissante $\mathcal{G}_{n-1} \subset \mathcal{G}_n$), d'où $\sigma(X_0,\ldots,X_n) \subset \mathcal{G}_n$.
Vérifier que $(\mathcal{F}_n = \sigma(X_0,\ldots,X_n))_{n}$ est bien une filtration ($\mathcal{F}_n \subset \mathcal{F}_{n+1}$) rendant $(X_n)$ adapté ($X_n$ est $\mathcal{F}_n$-mesurable).
Conclure que $\mathcal{F}_n \subset \mathcal{G}_n$ pour toute telle $(\mathcal{G}_n)$, ce qui établit la minimalité de $(\mathcal{F}_n)$.

front_en: **Exercise 2.** Let $(X_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ be a stochastic process, and set $\mathcal{F}_n = \sigma(X_0, \ldots, X_n)$ for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$. Check that $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ is the smallest filtration with respect to which $(X_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ is adapted.
back_en:
**Solution.** If $(\mathcal{G}_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ is a filtration with respect to which $(X_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ is adapted, then for each $n \in \mathbb{N}$, the random variables $X_0, \ldots, X_n$ are $\mathcal{G}_n$-measurable (indeed, $X_n$ is $\mathcal{G}_n$-measurable, $X_{n-1}$ is $\mathcal{G}_{n-1}$-measurable hence $\mathcal{G}_n$-measurable, etc.). Thus, we must have $\mathcal{G}_n \supset \sigma(X_0, \ldots, X_n)$.

Conversely, $(\mathcal{F}_n = \sigma(X_0, \ldots, X_n))_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ is a filtration with respect to which $(X_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ is adapted. $\square$

points.label: Lower bound: for any filtration $(\mathcal{G}_n)$ making $(X_n)$ adapted, show that $X_0, \ldots, X_n$ are all $\mathcal{G}_n$-measurable (via the increasing inclusion $\mathcal{G}_{n-1} \subset \mathcal{G}_n$), hence $\sigma(X_0,\ldots,X_n) \subset \mathcal{G}_n$.
Check that $(\mathcal{F}_n = \sigma(X_0,\ldots,X_n))_{n}$ is indeed a filtration ($\mathcal{F}_n \subset \mathcal{F}_{n+1}$) making $(X_n)$ adapted ($X_n$ is $\mathcal{F}_n$-measurable).
Conclude that $\mathcal{F}_n \subset \mathcal{G}_n$ for any such $(\mathcal{G}_n)$, which establishes the minimality of $(\mathcal{F}_n)$.

front:
**Exercice 3.** Soit $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ une filtration, et soit $(X_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ une martingale par rapport à $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$.

1. Montrer que pour tous $m \le n \in \mathbb{N}$, on a $\mathbb{E}[X_n \mid \mathcal{F}_m] = X_m$ presque sûrement.

2. Montrer que $(X_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ est une martingale par rapport à la filtration canonique $(\sigma(X_0, \ldots, X_n))_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$.

Quels sont les énoncés analogues pour une sous-martingale / sur-martingale ?
back:
**Solution.**

**1.** Fixons $m \in \mathbb{N}$, et montrons que pour tout $n \ge m$, on a $\mathbb{E}[X_n \mid \mathcal{F}_m] = X_m$ presque sûrement, par récurrence sur $n$. Pour $n = m$, il n'y a rien à faire. Fixons maintenant $n \ge m$ tel que $\mathbb{E}[X_n \mid \mathcal{F}_m] = X_m$ presque sûrement, et prouvons que $\mathbb{E}[X_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_m] = X_m$ presque sûrement.

En utilisant la propriété de la tour de l'espérance conditionnelle, puis la propriété de martingale $\mathbb{E}[X_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = X_n$ presque sûrement, on écrit
$$\mathbb{E}[X_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_m] = \mathbb{E}\big[\mathbb{E}[X_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] \mid \mathcal{F}_m\big] = \mathbb{E}[X_n \mid \mathcal{F}_m] = X_m \quad \text{presque sûrement,}$$
ce qui conclut la preuve par récurrence.

**2.** Pour chaque $n \in \mathbb{N}$, la variable aléatoire $X_n$ est mesurable par rapport à $\mathcal{G}_n := \sigma(X_0, \ldots, X_n)$ et intégrable. De plus, par la propriété de la tour de l'espérance conditionnelle, et puisque $X_n$ est $\mathcal{G}_n$-mesurable, on a
$$\mathbb{E}[X_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{G}_n] = \mathbb{E}\big[\mathbb{E}[X_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] \mid \mathcal{G}_n\big] = \mathbb{E}[X_n \mid \mathcal{G}_n] = X_n \quad \text{presque sûrement,}$$
ce qui montre que $(X_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ est une martingale par rapport à $(\mathcal{G}_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$.

points.label: Point 1 : preuve par récurrence sur $n \ge m$ que $\mathbb{E}[X_n \mid \mathcal{F}_m]=X_m$ p.s., avec initialisation $n=m$ triviale et étape d'hérédité utilisant la propriété de la tour $\mathbb{E}[X_{n+1}\mid\mathcal{F}_m]=\mathbb{E}[\mathbb{E}[X_{n+1}\mid\mathcal{F}_n]\mid\mathcal{F}_m]$ puis la propriété de martingale $\mathbb{E}[X_{n+1}\mid\mathcal{F}_n]=X_n$.

Point 2 : vérification que $X_n$ est $\mathcal{G}_n$-mesurable et intégrable (avec $\mathcal{G}_n=\sigma(X_0,\ldots,X_n)$), puis calcul $\mathbb{E}[X_{n+1}\mid\mathcal{G}_n]=\mathbb{E}[\mathbb{E}[X_{n+1}\mid\mathcal{F}_n]\mid\mathcal{G}_n]=\mathbb{E}[X_n\mid\mathcal{G}_n]=X_n$ p.s. (en notant que $\mathcal{G}_n\subseteq\mathcal{F}_n$ et $X_n$ est $\mathcal{G}_n$-mesurable).

Analogues sous-/sur-martingale : énoncer $\mathbb{E}[X_n\mid\mathcal{F}_m]\ge X_m$ (sous-martingale) resp. $\le X_m$ (sur-martingale) pour $m\le n$, et conserver la propriété par rapport à la filtration canonique.

front_en:
**Exercise 3.** Let $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ be a filtration, and let $(X_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ be a martingale with respect to $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$.

1. Show that for all $m \le n \in \mathbb{N}$, we have $\mathbb{E}[X_n \mid \mathcal{F}_m] = X_m$ almost surely.

2. Show that $(X_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ is a martingale with respect to the canonical filtration $(\sigma(X_0, \ldots, X_n))_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$.

What are the analogous statements for a submartingale / supermartingale?
back_en:
**Solution.**

**1.** Fix $m \in \mathbb{N}$, and let us show that for all $n \ge m$, we have $\mathbb{E}[X_n \mid \mathcal{F}_m] = X_m$ almost surely, by induction on $n$. For $n = m$, there is nothing to do. Now fix $n \ge m$ such that $\mathbb{E}[X_n \mid \mathcal{F}_m] = X_m$ almost surely, and let us prove that $\mathbb{E}[X_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_m] = X_m$ almost surely.

Using the tower property of conditional expectation, then the martingale property $\mathbb{E}[X_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = X_n$ almost surely, we write
$$\mathbb{E}[X_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_m] = \mathbb{E}\big[\mathbb{E}[X_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] \mid \mathcal{F}_m\big] = \mathbb{E}[X_n \mid \mathcal{F}_m] = X_m \quad \text{almost surely,}$$
which concludes the proof by induction.

**2.** For each $n \in \mathbb{N}$, the random variable $X_n$ is measurable with respect to $\mathcal{G}_n := \sigma(X_0, \ldots, X_n)$ and integrable. Moreover, by the tower property of conditional expectation, and since $X_n$ is $\mathcal{G}_n$-measurable, we have
$$\mathbb{E}[X_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{G}_n] = \mathbb{E}\big[\mathbb{E}[X_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] \mid \mathcal{G}_n\big] = \mathbb{E}[X_n \mid \mathcal{G}_n] = X_n \quad \text{almost surely,}$$
which shows that $(X_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ is a martingale with respect to $(\mathcal{G}_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$.

points.label: Part 1: proof by induction on $n \ge m$ that $\mathbb{E}[X_n \mid \mathcal{F}_m]=X_m$ a.s., with trivial initialization $n=m$ and an inductive step using the tower property $\mathbb{E}[X_{n+1}\mid\mathcal{F}_m]=\mathbb{E}[\mathbb{E}[X_{n+1}\mid\mathcal{F}_n]\mid\mathcal{F}_m]$ then the martingale property $\mathbb{E}[X_{n+1}\mid\mathcal{F}_n]=X_n$.

Part 2: check that $X_n$ is $\mathcal{G}_n$-measurable and integrable (with $\mathcal{G}_n=\sigma(X_0,\ldots,X_n)$), then compute $\mathbb{E}[X_{n+1}\mid\mathcal{G}_n]=\mathbb{E}[\mathbb{E}[X_{n+1}\mid\mathcal{F}_n]\mid\mathcal{G}_n]=\mathbb{E}[X_n\mid\mathcal{G}_n]=X_n$ a.s. (noting that $\mathcal{G}_n\subseteq\mathcal{F}_n$ and $X_n$ is $\mathcal{G}_n$-measurable).

Submartingale/supermartingale analogues: state $\mathbb{E}[X_n\mid\mathcal{F}_m]\ge X_m$ (submartingale) resp. $\le X_m$ (supermartingale) for $m\le n$, and that the property is preserved with respect to the canonical filtration.

front:
**Exercice 4** (Random walks). Soit $(X_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}^*}$ une suite de variables aléatoires indépendantes et identiquement distribuées. On pose $S_n = X_1 + \ldots + X_n$ pour tout $n \in \mathbb{N}$, et on note $\mathcal{F}_n = \sigma(X_1, \ldots, X_n)$.

1. Supposons que $\mathbb{E}[|X_1|] < \infty$. Vérifier que $(S_n - \mathbb{E}[X_1]n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ est une martingale par rapport à $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$.

2. Supposons que $\mathbb{E}[X_1^2] < \infty$, et soit $\sigma^2 = \mathbb{E}[(X_1 - \mathbb{E}[X_1])^2]$ la variance de $X_1$. Vérifier que $\big((S_n - \mathbb{E}[X_1]n)^2 - \sigma^2 n\big)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ est une martingale par rapport à $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$.

3. Fixons $\lambda \in \mathbb{R}$ tel que $\mathbb{E}[e^{\lambda X_1}] < \infty$. Vérifier que $(\exp(\lambda S_n - L(\lambda)n))_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ est une martingale par rapport à $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$, où $L(\lambda) = \ln\big(\mathbb{E}[e^{\lambda X_1}]\big)$.
back:
**Solution.**

**1.** Pour chaque $n \in \mathbb{N}$, la variable aléatoire $S_n - \mathbb{E}[X_1]n$ est $\mathcal{F}_n$-mesurable comme combinaison linéaire mesurable de $X_1, \ldots, X_n$, et intégrable comme combinaison linéaire de variables intégrables. De plus, puisque $S_n$ est $\mathcal{F}_n$-mesurable et $X_{n+1}$ est indépendante de $\mathcal{F}_n$, on a
$$\mathbb{E}[S_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = \mathbb{E}[S_n + X_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = S_n + \mathbb{E}[X_{n+1}] = S_n + \mathbb{E}[X_1] \quad \text{presque sûrement.}$$
En soustrayant $\mathbb{E}[X_1](n+1)$ des deux côtés, on obtient
$$\mathbb{E}[S_{n+1} - \mathbb{E}[X_1](n+1) \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = S_n - \mathbb{E}[X_1]n \quad \text{presque sûrement,}$$
ce qui montre que $(S_n - \mathbb{E}[X_1]n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ est une martingale par rapport à $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$.

**2.** Posons $Y_n = X_n - \mathbb{E}[X_1]$ pour tout $n \in \mathbb{N}^*$, et soit $T_n = Y_1 + \ldots + Y_n = S_n - \mathbb{E}[X_1]n$ pour tout $n \in \mathbb{N}$. On veut montrer que $\big(T_n^2 - \sigma^2 n\big)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ est une martingale par rapport à $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$. Pour chaque $n \in \mathbb{N}$, la variable aléatoire $T_n^2 - \sigma^2 n$ est $\mathcal{F}_n$-mesurable comme fonction mesurable de $X_1, \ldots, X_n$, et intégrable puisque $S_n$ est de carré intégrable (comme somme de variables de carré intégrable). De plus, comme $T_n$ est $\mathcal{F}_n$-mesurable et $Y_{n+1}$ est indépendante de $\mathcal{F}_n$, on a $T_{n+1}^2 = (T_n + Y_{n+1})^2 = T_n^2 + 2 T_n Y_{n+1} + Y_{n+1}^2$. Ainsi,
$$\mathbb{E}[T_{n+1}^2 \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = \mathbb{E}[T_n^2 + 2 T_n Y_{n+1} + Y_{n+1}^2 \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = T_n^2 + 2 T_n \cdot \mathbb{E}[Y_{n+1}] + \mathbb{E}[Y_{n+1}^2] = T_n^2 + \sigma^2$$
presque sûrement (car $\mathbb{E}[Y_{n+1}] = 0$ et $\mathbb{E}[Y_{n+1}^2] = \sigma^2$). En soustrayant $\sigma^2(n+1)$ des deux côtés, on obtient
$$\mathbb{E}[T_{n+1}^2 - \sigma^2(n+1) \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = T_n^2 - \sigma^2 n \quad \text{presque sûrement,}$$
ce qui conclut la preuve.

**3.** Pour chaque $n \in \mathbb{N}$, la variable aléatoire $M_n := e^{\lambda S_n - L(\lambda)n}$ est $\mathcal{F}_n$-mesurable comme fonction mesurable de $S_n$, qui est $\mathcal{F}_n$-mesurable. De plus, $M_{n+1}$ étant positive, il est licite de vérifier la propriété de martingale $\mathbb{E}[M_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = M_n$ avant même de justifier que les $M_n$ sont intégrables. Puisque $e^{\lambda S_n}$ est $\mathcal{F}_n$-mesurable et $e^{\lambda X_{n+1}}$ est indépendante de $\mathcal{F}_n$, on a
$$\mathbb{E}[e^{\lambda S_{n+1}} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = \mathbb{E}[e^{\lambda S_n} \cdot e^{\lambda X_{n+1}} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = e^{\lambda S_n} \cdot \mathbb{E}[e^{\lambda X_{n+1}}] = e^{\lambda S_n} \cdot e^{L(\lambda)}$$
presque sûrement. En divisant par $e^{L(\lambda)(n+1)}$ des deux côtés, on obtient
$$\mathbb{E}[e^{\lambda S_{n+1} - L(\lambda)(n+1)} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = e^{\lambda S_n - L(\lambda)n} \quad \text{presque sûrement,}$$
comme voulu. En particulier, il s'ensuit que $\mathbb{E}[M_{n+1}] = \mathbb{E}[M_n]$, ce qui donne $\mathbb{E}[M_n] = \mathbb{E}[M_0] = 1$ pour tout $n \in \mathbb{N}$. Ainsi, les variables aléatoires $(M_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ sont intégrables, et on conclut que le processus $(M_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ est une martingale par rapport à $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$. $\square$

points.label: Partie 1 : montrer adaptation et intégrabilité de $S_n - \mathbb{E}[X_1]n$, puis utiliser l'indépendance de $X_{n+1}$ et $\mathcal{F}_n$ pour obtenir $\mathbb{E}[S_{n+1}\mid\mathcal{F}_n] = S_n + \mathbb{E}[X_1]$, et conclure en soustrayant $\mathbb{E}[X_1](n+1)$.

Partie 2 : poser $T_n = S_n - \mathbb{E}[X_1]n$, développer $T_{n+1}^2 = T_n^2 + 2T_n Y_{n+1} + Y_{n+1}^2$, et calculer $\mathbb{E}[T_{n+1}^2\mid\mathcal{F}_n] = T_n^2 + \sigma^2$ via $\mathbb{E}[Y_{n+1}]=0$ et $\mathbb{E}[Y_{n+1}^2]=\sigma^2$, puis soustraire $\sigma^2(n+1)$.

Partie 3 : factoriser $e^{\lambda S_{n+1}} = e^{\lambda S_n} e^{\lambda X_{n+1}}$, utiliser l'indépendance pour obtenir $\mathbb{E}[e^{\lambda S_{n+1}}\mid\mathcal{F}_n] = e^{\lambda S_n} e^{L(\lambda)}$, diviser par $e^{L(\lambda)(n+1)}$ et conclure $\mathbb{E}[M_n]=1$ (intégrabilité).

front_en:
**Exercise 4** (Random walks). Let $(X_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}^*}$ be a sequence of independent and identically distributed random variables. Set $S_n = X_1 + \ldots + X_n$ for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$, and write $\mathcal{F}_n = \sigma(X_1, \ldots, X_n)$.

1. Assume that $\mathbb{E}[|X_1|] < \infty$. Check that $(S_n - \mathbb{E}[X_1]n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ is a martingale with respect to $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$.

2. Assume that $\mathbb{E}[X_1^2] < \infty$, and let $\sigma^2 = \mathbb{E}[(X_1 - \mathbb{E}[X_1])^2]$ be the variance of $X_1$. Check that $\big((S_n - \mathbb{E}[X_1]n)^2 - \sigma^2 n\big)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ is a martingale with respect to $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$.

3. Fix $\lambda \in \mathbb{R}$ such that $\mathbb{E}[e^{\lambda X_1}] < \infty$. Check that $(\exp(\lambda S_n - L(\lambda)n))_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ is a martingale with respect to $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$, where $L(\lambda) = \ln\big(\mathbb{E}[e^{\lambda X_1}]\big)$.
back_en:
**Solution.**

**1.** For each $n \in \mathbb{N}$, the random variable $S_n - \mathbb{E}[X_1]n$ is $\mathcal{F}_n$-measurable as a measurable linear combination of $X_1, \ldots, X_n$, and integrable as a linear combination of integrable variables. Moreover, since $S_n$ is $\mathcal{F}_n$-measurable and $X_{n+1}$ is independent of $\mathcal{F}_n$, we have
$$\mathbb{E}[S_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = \mathbb{E}[S_n + X_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = S_n + \mathbb{E}[X_{n+1}] = S_n + \mathbb{E}[X_1] \quad \text{almost surely.}$$
Subtracting $\mathbb{E}[X_1](n+1)$ from both sides, we obtain
$$\mathbb{E}[S_{n+1} - \mathbb{E}[X_1](n+1) \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = S_n - \mathbb{E}[X_1]n \quad \text{almost surely,}$$
which shows that $(S_n - \mathbb{E}[X_1]n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ is a martingale with respect to $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$.

**2.** Set $Y_n = X_n - \mathbb{E}[X_1]$ for all $n \in \mathbb{N}^*$, and let $T_n = Y_1 + \ldots + Y_n = S_n - \mathbb{E}[X_1]n$ for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$. We want to show that $\big(T_n^2 - \sigma^2 n\big)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ is a martingale with respect to $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$. For each $n \in \mathbb{N}$, the random variable $T_n^2 - \sigma^2 n$ is $\mathcal{F}_n$-measurable as a measurable function of $X_1, \ldots, X_n$, and integrable since $S_n$ is square-integrable (as a sum of square-integrable variables). Moreover, since $T_n$ is $\mathcal{F}_n$-measurable and $Y_{n+1}$ is independent of $\mathcal{F}_n$, we have $T_{n+1}^2 = (T_n + Y_{n+1})^2 = T_n^2 + 2 T_n Y_{n+1} + Y_{n+1}^2$. Thus,
$$\mathbb{E}[T_{n+1}^2 \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = \mathbb{E}[T_n^2 + 2 T_n Y_{n+1} + Y_{n+1}^2 \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = T_n^2 + 2 T_n \cdot \mathbb{E}[Y_{n+1}] + \mathbb{E}[Y_{n+1}^2] = T_n^2 + \sigma^2$$
almost surely (since $\mathbb{E}[Y_{n+1}] = 0$ and $\mathbb{E}[Y_{n+1}^2] = \sigma^2$). Subtracting $\sigma^2(n+1)$ from both sides, we obtain
$$\mathbb{E}[T_{n+1}^2 - \sigma^2(n+1) \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = T_n^2 - \sigma^2 n \quad \text{almost surely,}$$
which concludes the proof.

**3.** For each $n \in \mathbb{N}$, the random variable $M_n := e^{\lambda S_n - L(\lambda)n}$ is $\mathcal{F}_n$-measurable as a measurable function of $S_n$, which is $\mathcal{F}_n$-measurable. Moreover, since $M_{n+1}$ is nonnegative, it is legitimate to check the martingale property $\mathbb{E}[M_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = M_n$ even before justifying that the $M_n$ are integrable. Since $e^{\lambda S_n}$ is $\mathcal{F}_n$-measurable and $e^{\lambda X_{n+1}}$ is independent of $\mathcal{F}_n$, we have
$$\mathbb{E}[e^{\lambda S_{n+1}} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = \mathbb{E}[e^{\lambda S_n} \cdot e^{\lambda X_{n+1}} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = e^{\lambda S_n} \cdot \mathbb{E}[e^{\lambda X_{n+1}}] = e^{\lambda S_n} \cdot e^{L(\lambda)}$$
almost surely. Dividing both sides by $e^{L(\lambda)(n+1)}$, we obtain
$$\mathbb{E}[e^{\lambda S_{n+1} - L(\lambda)(n+1)} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = e^{\lambda S_n - L(\lambda)n} \quad \text{almost surely,}$$
as desired. In particular, it follows that $\mathbb{E}[M_{n+1}] = \mathbb{E}[M_n]$, which gives $\mathbb{E}[M_n] = \mathbb{E}[M_0] = 1$ for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$. Thus, the random variables $(M_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ are integrable, and we conclude that the process $(M_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ is a martingale with respect to $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$. $\square$

points.label: Part 1: show adaptedness and integrability of $S_n - \mathbb{E}[X_1]n$, then use the independence of $X_{n+1}$ and $\mathcal{F}_n$ to obtain $\mathbb{E}[S_{n+1}\mid\mathcal{F}_n] = S_n + \mathbb{E}[X_1]$, and conclude by subtracting $\mathbb{E}[X_1](n+1)$.

Part 2: set $T_n = S_n - \mathbb{E}[X_1]n$, expand $T_{n+1}^2 = T_n^2 + 2T_n Y_{n+1} + Y_{n+1}^2$, and compute $\mathbb{E}[T_{n+1}^2\mid\mathcal{F}_n] = T_n^2 + \sigma^2$ via $\mathbb{E}[Y_{n+1}]=0$ and $\mathbb{E}[Y_{n+1}^2]=\sigma^2$, then subtract $\sigma^2(n+1)$.

Part 3: factorize $e^{\lambda S_{n+1}} = e^{\lambda S_n} e^{\lambda X_{n+1}}$, use independence to obtain $\mathbb{E}[e^{\lambda S_{n+1}}\mid\mathcal{F}_n] = e^{\lambda S_n} e^{L(\lambda)}$, divide by $e^{L(\lambda)(n+1)}$ and conclude $\mathbb{E}[M_n]=1$ (integrability).

front:
**Exercice 5** (Processus de Bienaymé). Fixons une mesure de probabilité discrète $\mu$ sur $\mathbb{N}$ telle que
$$m := \sum_{k \geq 0} k \cdot \mu(\{k\}) < \infty,$$
et soit $(\xi_i^n)_{n, i \in \mathbb{N}^*}$ une collection de variables aléatoires indépendantes de loi $\mu$. Soit alors $(Z_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ le processus stochastique donné par $Z_0 = 1$, et
$$Z_{n+1} = \sum_{i=1}^{Z_n} \xi_i^{n+1} \quad \text{pour tout } n \in \mathbb{N},$$
et posons $\mathcal{F}_n = \sigma\!\left(\xi_i^k \,;\, k \in [\![1, n]\!] \text{ et } i \in \mathbb{N}^*\right)$. Vérifier que le processus
$$\left(W_n = \frac{Z_n}{m^n}\right)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$$
est une martingale par rapport à $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$.
back:
**Solution.** Pour chaque $n \in \mathbb{N}$, la variable aléatoire $W_n$ est $\mathcal{F}_n$-mesurable comme fonction mesurable de $Z_n$ qui est $\mathcal{F}_n$-mesurable (voir la première série d'exercices). De plus, comme $W_{n+1}$ est positive, on peut vérifier la propriété de martingale $\mathbb{E}[W_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = W_n$ avant de justifier que les $W_n$ sont intégrables. Comme $Z_n$ est $\mathcal{F}_n$-mesurable et $(\xi_i^{n+1})_{i \in \mathbb{N}^*}$ est indépendante de $\mathcal{F}_n$, on a
$$\mathbb{E}[Z_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = \mathbb{E}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{Z_n} \xi_i^{n+1} \,\Big|\, \mathcal{F}_n\right] = \sum_{i=1}^{Z_n} \mathbb{E}[\xi_i^{n+1}] = Z_n \cdot m \quad \text{presque sûrement.}$$

En effet, on a $Z_{n+1} = \varphi\!\left(Z_n, (\xi_i^{n+1})_{i \in \mathbb{N}^*}\right)$, où $\varphi : \mathbb{N} \times \mathbb{N}^{\mathbb{N}^*} \to \mathbb{R}_+$ est donnée par
$$\varphi\!\left(z, (k_i)_{i \in \mathbb{N}^*}\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{z} k_i \quad \text{pour tout } (z, (k_i)_{i \in \mathbb{N}^*}) \in \mathbb{N} \times \mathbb{N}^{\mathbb{N}^*}.$$

Ainsi, par le Lemme 2.3 du polycopié, on obtient
$$\mathbb{E}[Z_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = \mathbb{E}\left[\varphi\!\left(Z_n, (\xi_i^{n+1})_{i \in \mathbb{N}^*}\right) \,\Big|\, \mathcal{F}_n\right] = \Phi(Z_n) \quad \text{presque sûrement,}$$
où $\Phi : \mathbb{N} \to [0, \infty]$ est donnée par
$$\Phi(z) = \mathbb{E}\left[\varphi\!\left(z, (\xi_i^{n+1})_{i \in \mathbb{N}^*}\right)\right] = \mathbb{E}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{z} \xi_i^{n+1}\right] = z \cdot m \quad \text{pour tout } z \in \mathbb{N}.$$

En divisant les deux membres par $m^{n+1}$ dans $\mathbb{E}[Z_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = Z_n \cdot m$ presque sûrement, on obtient $\mathbb{E}[W_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = W_n$ presque sûrement, comme voulu. En particulier, il s'ensuit que $\mathbb{E}[W_n] = \mathbb{E}[W_0] = 1$ pour tout $n \in \mathbb{N}$. Ainsi, les variables aléatoires $(W_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ sont intégrables, et on conclut que le processus $(W_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ est une martingale par rapport à $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$. $\square$

points.label: Mesurabilité et intégrabilité : $W_n$ est $\mathcal{F}_n$-mesurable (car fonction de $Z_n$), et $\mathbb{E}[W_n] = \mathbb{E}[W_0] = 1 < \infty$ pour tout $n$, donc $W_n$ intégrable.

Calcul conditionnel : écrire $Z_{n+1} = \varphi(Z_n, (\xi_i^{n+1})_i)$ avec $Z_n$ $\mathcal{F}_n$-mesurable et $(\xi_i^{n+1})_i$ indépendante de $\mathcal{F}_n$, et appliquer le lemme de gel/conditionnement pour obtenir $\mathbb{E}[Z_{n+1}\mid\mathcal{F}_n] = \Phi(Z_n)$ avec $\Phi(z) = \mathbb{E}[\sum_{i=1}^z \xi_i^{n+1}] = z\cdot m$, d'où $\mathbb{E}[Z_{n+1}\mid\mathcal{F}_n] = m\,Z_n$ p.s.
Conclusion : diviser par $m^{n+1}$ pour obtenir $\mathbb{E}[W_{n+1}\mid\mathcal{F}_n] = W_n$ p.s., donc $(W_n)$ est une martingale par rapport à $(\mathcal{F}_n)$.

front_en:
**Exercise 5** (Bienaymé processes). Fix a discrete probability measure $\mu$ on $\mathbb{N}$ such that
$$m := \sum_{k \geq 0} k \cdot \mu(\{k\}) < \infty,$$
and let $(\xi_i^n)_{n, i \in \mathbb{N}^*}$ be a collection of independent random variables with law $\mu$. Then let $(Z_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ be the stochastic process given by $Z_0 = 1$, and
$$Z_{n+1} = \sum_{i=1}^{Z_n} \xi_i^{n+1} \quad \text{for all } n \in \mathbb{N},$$
and set $\mathcal{F}_n = \sigma\!\left(\xi_i^k \,;\, k \in [\![1, n]\!] \text{ and } i \in \mathbb{N}^*\right)$. Check that the process
$$\left(W_n = \frac{Z_n}{m^n}\right)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$$
is a martingale with respect to $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$.
back_en:
**Solution.** For each $n \in \mathbb{N}$, the random variable $W_n$ is $\mathcal{F}_n$-measurable as a measurable function of $Z_n$, which is $\mathcal{F}_n$-measurable (see the first exercise sheet). Moreover, since $W_{n+1}$ is nonnegative, we may check the martingale property $\mathbb{E}[W_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = W_n$ before justifying that the $W_n$ are integrable. Since $Z_n$ is $\mathcal{F}_n$-measurable and $(\xi_i^{n+1})_{i \in \mathbb{N}^*}$ is independent of $\mathcal{F}_n$, we have
$$\mathbb{E}[Z_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = \mathbb{E}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{Z_n} \xi_i^{n+1} \,\Big|\, \mathcal{F}_n\right] = \sum_{i=1}^{Z_n} \mathbb{E}[\xi_i^{n+1}] = Z_n \cdot m \quad \text{almost surely.}$$

Indeed, we have $Z_{n+1} = \varphi\!\left(Z_n, (\xi_i^{n+1})_{i \in \mathbb{N}^*}\right)$, where $\varphi : \mathbb{N} \times \mathbb{N}^{\mathbb{N}^*} \to \mathbb{R}_+$ is given by
$$\varphi\!\left(z, (k_i)_{i \in \mathbb{N}^*}\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{z} k_i \quad \text{for all } (z, (k_i)_{i \in \mathbb{N}^*}) \in \mathbb{N} \times \mathbb{N}^{\mathbb{N}^*}.$$

Thus, by Lemma 2.3 in the lecture notes, we obtain
$$\mathbb{E}[Z_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = \mathbb{E}\left[\varphi\!\left(Z_n, (\xi_i^{n+1})_{i \in \mathbb{N}^*}\right) \,\Big|\, \mathcal{F}_n\right] = \Phi(Z_n) \quad \text{almost surely,}$$
where $\Phi : \mathbb{N} \to [0, \infty]$ is given by
$$\Phi(z) = \mathbb{E}\left[\varphi\!\left(z, (\xi_i^{n+1})_{i \in \mathbb{N}^*}\right)\right] = \mathbb{E}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{z} \xi_i^{n+1}\right] = z \cdot m \quad \text{for all } z \in \mathbb{N}.$$

Dividing both sides by $m^{n+1}$ in $\mathbb{E}[Z_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = Z_n \cdot m$ almost surely, we obtain $\mathbb{E}[W_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = W_n$ almost surely, as desired. In particular, it follows that $\mathbb{E}[W_n] = \mathbb{E}[W_0] = 1$ for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$. Thus, the random variables $(W_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ are integrable, and we conclude that the process $(W_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ is a martingale with respect to $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$. $\square$

points.label: Measurability and integrability: $W_n$ is $\mathcal{F}_n$-measurable (as a function of $Z_n$), and $\mathbb{E}[W_n] = \mathbb{E}[W_0] = 1 < \infty$ for all $n$, hence $W_n$ is integrable.

Conditional computation: write $Z_{n+1} = \varphi(Z_n, (\xi_i^{n+1})_i)$ with $Z_n$ $\mathcal{F}_n$-measurable and $(\xi_i^{n+1})_i$ independent of $\mathcal{F}_n$, and apply the freezing/conditioning lemma to obtain $\mathbb{E}[Z_{n+1}\mid\mathcal{F}_n] = \Phi(Z_n)$ with $\Phi(z) = \mathbb{E}[\sum_{i=1}^z \xi_i^{n+1}] = z\cdot m$, hence $\mathbb{E}[Z_{n+1}\mid\mathcal{F}_n] = m\,Z_n$ a.s.
Conclusion: divide by $m^{n+1}$ to obtain $\mathbb{E}[W_{n+1}\mid\mathcal{F}_n] = W_n$ a.s., so $(W_n)$ is a martingale with respect to $(\mathcal{F}_n)$.

front:
**Exercise 6** (Pólya urns). Fix $a, b \in \mathbb{N}^*$, and let $(U_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}^*}$ be a sequence of independent random variables with uniform distribution on $[0,1]$. Then, let $(R_n, B_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ be the stochastic process constructed as follows. Initially, set $R_0 = a$ and $B_0 = b$, and by induction, for each $n \in \mathbb{N}$ such that $R_n, B_n$ have been constructed, construct $R_{n+1}, B_{n+1}$ as follows:

- If $U_{n+1} \le R_n/(R_n + B_n)$, then set $R_{n+1} = R_n + 1$ and $B_{n+1} = B_n$,
- If $U_{n+1} > R_n/(R_n + B_n)$, then set $R_{n+1} = R_n$ and $B_{n+1} = B_n + 1$.

**1.** Check that the process
$$\left( M_n = \frac{R_n}{R_n + B_n} \right)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$$
is a martingale with respect to $(\mathcal{F}_n = \sigma(U_1, \ldots, U_n))_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$.

**2.** More generally, check that for each $k \in \mathbb{N}^*$, the process
$$\left( M_n = \frac{R_n(R_n+1)\ldots(R_n + k - 1)}{(R_n + B_n)\ldots(R_n + B_n + k - 1)} \right)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$$
is a martingale with respect to $(\mathcal{F}_n = \sigma(U_1, \ldots, U_n))_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$.
back:
**Solution.**

**1.** For each $n \in \mathbb{N}$, the random variable $M_n$ is $\mathcal{F}_n$-measurable as a measurable function of $(R_n, B_n)$ which is $\mathcal{F}_n$-measurable (see the first exercise sheet), and integrable since bounded. Finally, let us check the martingale property. Note that
$$M_{n+1} = \begin{cases} \dfrac{R_n + 1}{R_n + B_n + 1} & \text{if } U_{n+1} \le \dfrac{R_n}{R_n + B_n}, \\[2mm] \dfrac{R_n}{R_n + B_n + 1} & \text{if } U_{n+1} > \dfrac{R_n}{R_n + B_n}. \end{cases}$$

Thus, since $(R_n, B_n)$ is $\mathcal{F}_n$-measurable and $U_{n+1}$ is independent of $\mathcal{F}_n$, we get
$$\mathbb{E}[M_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = \frac{R_n + 1}{R_n + B_n + 1} \cdot \frac{R_n}{R_n + B_n} + \frac{R_n}{R_n + B_n + 1} \cdot \frac{B_n}{R_n + B_n}.$$

Indeed, we have $M_{n+1} = \varphi((R_n, B_n), U_{n+1})$, where $\varphi : (\mathbb{N}^* \times \mathbb{N}^*) \times [0,1] \to \mathbb{R}_+$ is given by
$$\varphi((r, b), u) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{r+1}{r+b+1} & \text{if } u \le \dfrac{r}{r+b}, \\[2mm] \dfrac{r}{r+b+1} & \text{if } u > \dfrac{r}{r+b}, \end{cases} \qquad \text{for all } ((r,b), u) \in (\mathbb{N}^* \times \mathbb{N}^*) \times [0,1].$$

Thus, by Lemma 2.3 in the notes, we get
$$\mathbb{E}[M_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = \mathbb{E}[\varphi((R_n, B_n), U_{n+1}) \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = \Phi(R_n, B_n) \quad \text{almost surely,}$$
where $\Phi : \mathbb{N}^* \times \mathbb{N}^* \to [0, \infty]$ is given by
$$\Phi(r, b) = \mathbb{E}[\varphi((r, b), U_{n+1})] = \frac{r+1}{r+b+1} \cdot \frac{r}{r+b} + \frac{r}{r+b+1} \cdot \frac{b}{r+b} \quad \text{for all } (r, b) \in \mathbb{N}^* \times \mathbb{N}^*.$$

We conclude that
$$\mathbb{E}[M_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = \frac{R_n + 1}{R_n + B_n + 1} \cdot \frac{R_n}{R_n + B_n} + \frac{R_n}{R_n + B_n + 1} \cdot \frac{B_n}{R_n + B_n} = \frac{R_n(R_n + B_n + 1)}{(R_n + B_n)(R_n + B_n + 1)} = \frac{R_n}{R_n + B_n} = M_n \quad \text{almost surely,}$$
as desired.

**2.** The argument for general $k \in \mathbb{N}^*$ is very similar, we only show the martingale property here: almost surely, we have
$$\mathbb{E}[M_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = \frac{(R_n + 1)\ldots(R_n + k)}{(R_n + B_n + 1)\ldots(R_n + B_n + k)} \cdot \frac{R_n}{R_n + B_n} + \frac{R_n(R_n + 1)\ldots(R_n + k - 1)}{(R_n + B_n + 1)\ldots(R_n + B_n + k)} \cdot \frac{B_n}{R_n + B_n}$$
$$= \frac{R_n(R_n + 1)\ldots(R_n + k - 1)(R_n + B_n + k)}{(R_n + B_n)(R_n + B_n + 1)\ldots(R_n + B_n + k)} = \frac{R_n(R_n + 1)\ldots(R_n + k - 1)}{(R_n + B_n)\ldots(R_n + B_n + k - 1)} = M_n,$$
as desired.

points.label: Adaptation et intégrabilité : justifier que $M_n$ est $\mathcal{F}_n$-mesurable (fonction mesurable de $(R_n,B_n)$ qui est $\mathcal{F}_n$-mesurable) et intégrable (bornée).

Question 1 : écrire les deux cas pour $M_{n+1}$ ($\frac{R_n+1}{R_n+B_n+1}$ avec proba $\frac{R_n}{R_n+B_n}$, et $\frac{R_n}{R_n+B_n+1}$ avec proba $\frac{B_n}{R_n+B_n}$), utiliser l'indépendance de $U_{n+1}$ et $\mathcal{F}_n$ (Lemme 2.3) pour calculer $\mathbb{E}[M_{n+1}\mid\mathcal{F}_n]$ et simplifier le numérateur $R_n(R_n+B_n+1)$ en $M_n=\frac{R_n}{R_n+B_n}$.

Question 2 : reproduire le calcul de $\mathbb{E}[M_{n+1}\mid\mathcal{F}_n]$ pour le produit général d'ordre $k$, factoriser $R_n(R_n+1)\ldots(R_n+k-1)$ et faire apparaître le facteur $(R_n+B_n+k)$ au numérateur pour le simplifier et retrouver $M_n$.

front_en:
**Exercise 6** (Pólya urns). Fix $a, b \in \mathbb{N}^*$, and let $(U_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}^*}$ be a sequence of independent random variables with uniform distribution on $[0,1]$. Then, let $(R_n, B_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ be the stochastic process constructed as follows. Initially, set $R_0 = a$ and $B_0 = b$, and by induction, for each $n \in \mathbb{N}$ such that $R_n, B_n$ have been constructed, construct $R_{n+1}, B_{n+1}$ as follows:

- If $U_{n+1} \le R_n/(R_n + B_n)$, then set $R_{n+1} = R_n + 1$ and $B_{n+1} = B_n$,
- If $U_{n+1} > R_n/(R_n + B_n)$, then set $R_{n+1} = R_n$ and $B_{n+1} = B_n + 1$.

**1.** Check that the process
$$\left( M_n = \frac{R_n}{R_n + B_n} \right)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$$
is a martingale with respect to $(\mathcal{F}_n = \sigma(U_1, \ldots, U_n))_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$.

**2.** More generally, check that for each $k \in \mathbb{N}^*$, the process
$$\left( M_n = \frac{R_n(R_n+1)\ldots(R_n + k - 1)}{(R_n + B_n)\ldots(R_n + B_n + k - 1)} \right)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$$
is a martingale with respect to $(\mathcal{F}_n = \sigma(U_1, \ldots, U_n))_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$.
back_en:
**Solution.**

**1.** For each $n \in \mathbb{N}$, the random variable $M_n$ is $\mathcal{F}_n$-measurable as a measurable function of $(R_n, B_n)$ which is $\mathcal{F}_n$-measurable (see the first exercise sheet), and integrable since bounded. Finally, let us check the martingale property. Note that
$$M_{n+1} = \begin{cases} \dfrac{R_n + 1}{R_n + B_n + 1} & \text{if } U_{n+1} \le \dfrac{R_n}{R_n + B_n}, \\[2mm] \dfrac{R_n}{R_n + B_n + 1} & \text{if } U_{n+1} > \dfrac{R_n}{R_n + B_n}. \end{cases}$$

Thus, since $(R_n, B_n)$ is $\mathcal{F}_n$-measurable and $U_{n+1}$ is independent of $\mathcal{F}_n$, we get
$$\mathbb{E}[M_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = \frac{R_n + 1}{R_n + B_n + 1} \cdot \frac{R_n}{R_n + B_n} + \frac{R_n}{R_n + B_n + 1} \cdot \frac{B_n}{R_n + B_n}.$$

Indeed, we have $M_{n+1} = \varphi((R_n, B_n), U_{n+1})$, where $\varphi : (\mathbb{N}^* \times \mathbb{N}^*) \times [0,1] \to \mathbb{R}_+$ is given by
$$\varphi((r, b), u) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{r+1}{r+b+1} & \text{if } u \le \dfrac{r}{r+b}, \\[2mm] \dfrac{r}{r+b+1} & \text{if } u > \dfrac{r}{r+b}, \end{cases} \qquad \text{for all } ((r,b), u) \in (\mathbb{N}^* \times \mathbb{N}^*) \times [0,1].$$

Thus, by Lemma 2.3 in the notes, we get
$$\mathbb{E}[M_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = \mathbb{E}[\varphi((R_n, B_n), U_{n+1}) \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = \Phi(R_n, B_n) \quad \text{almost surely,}$$
where $\Phi : \mathbb{N}^* \times \mathbb{N}^* \to [0, \infty]$ is given by
$$\Phi(r, b) = \mathbb{E}[\varphi((r, b), U_{n+1})] = \frac{r+1}{r+b+1} \cdot \frac{r}{r+b} + \frac{r}{r+b+1} \cdot \frac{b}{r+b} \quad \text{for all } (r, b) \in \mathbb{N}^* \times \mathbb{N}^*.$$

We conclude that
$$\mathbb{E}[M_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = \frac{R_n + 1}{R_n + B_n + 1} \cdot \frac{R_n}{R_n + B_n} + \frac{R_n}{R_n + B_n + 1} \cdot \frac{B_n}{R_n + B_n} = \frac{R_n(R_n + B_n + 1)}{(R_n + B_n)(R_n + B_n + 1)} = \frac{R_n}{R_n + B_n} = M_n \quad \text{almost surely,}$$
as desired.

**2.** The argument for general $k \in \mathbb{N}^*$ is very similar, we only show the martingale property here: almost surely, we have
$$\mathbb{E}[M_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = \frac{(R_n + 1)\ldots(R_n + k)}{(R_n + B_n + 1)\ldots(R_n + B_n + k)} \cdot \frac{R_n}{R_n + B_n} + \frac{R_n(R_n + 1)\ldots(R_n + k - 1)}{(R_n + B_n + 1)\ldots(R_n + B_n + k)} \cdot \frac{B_n}{R_n + B_n}$$
$$= \frac{R_n(R_n + 1)\ldots(R_n + k - 1)(R_n + B_n + k)}{(R_n + B_n)(R_n + B_n + 1)\ldots(R_n + B_n + k)} = \frac{R_n(R_n + 1)\ldots(R_n + k - 1)}{(R_n + B_n)\ldots(R_n + B_n + k - 1)} = M_n,$$
as desired.

points.label: Adaptedness and integrability: justify that $M_n$ is $\mathcal{F}_n$-measurable (measurable function of $(R_n,B_n)$ which is $\mathcal{F}_n$-measurable) and integrable (bounded).

Question 1: write the two cases for $M_{n+1}$ ($\frac{R_n+1}{R_n+B_n+1}$ with probability $\frac{R_n}{R_n+B_n}$, and $\frac{R_n}{R_n+B_n+1}$ with probability $\frac{B_n}{R_n+B_n}$), use the independence of $U_{n+1}$ and $\mathcal{F}_n$ (Lemma 2.3) to compute $\mathbb{E}[M_{n+1}\mid\mathcal{F}_n]$ and simplify the numerator $R_n(R_n+B_n+1)$ to $M_n=\frac{R_n}{R_n+B_n}$.

Question 2: reproduce the computation of $\mathbb{E}[M_{n+1}\mid\mathcal{F}_n]$ for the general product of order $k$, factor out $R_n(R_n+1)\ldots(R_n+k-1)$ and bring out the factor $(R_n+B_n+k)$ in the numerator to simplify it and recover $M_n$.

front:
**Exercise 7 (Doob decomposition).** Let $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ be a filtration, let $(X_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ be an adapted process, and assume that $\mathbb{E}[|X_n|] < \infty$ for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$.

1. Prove that there exists a unique decomposition
$$X_n = X_0 + A_n + M_n \quad \text{for all } n \in \mathbb{N},$$
where:
- $A_0 = 0$, and $(A_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}^*}$ is predictable with respect to $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$,
- $M_0 = 0$, and $(M_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ is a martingale with respect to $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$.

2. Prove that $(X_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ is a submartingale with respect to $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ if and only if for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$, we have $A_{n+1} \ge A_n$ almost surely. What is the analogous statement for a supermartingale?
back:
**Solution.**

**1.** *(Uniqueness.)* Let $(A_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ and $(M_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ be such a decomposition of $(X_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$. For each $n \in \mathbb{N}$, we have
$$X_{n+1} = X_0 + A_{n+1} + M_{n+1},$$
where $X_{n+1}$, $X_0$ and $M_{n+1}$ are integrable. Thus, the random variable
$$A_{n+1} = X_{n+1} - X_0 - M_{n+1}$$
is integrable, and we can take the conditional expectation given $\mathcal{F}_n$ in $X_{n+1} = X_0 + A_{n+1} + M_{n+1}$.

Since $X_0$ and $A_{n+1}$ are $\mathcal{F}_n$-measurable, and since $(M_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ is a martingale with respect to $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$, we get
$$\mathbb{E}[X_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = X_0 + A_{n+1} + M_n = X_0 + A_{n+1} + (X_n - X_0 - A_n) \quad \text{almost surely},$$
i.e.,
$$A_{n+1} - A_n = \mathbb{E}[X_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] - X_n \quad \text{almost surely}.$$
Since $A_0 = 0$, it follows that
$$A_n = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \big(\mathbb{E}[X_k \mid \mathcal{F}_{k-1}] - X_{k-1}\big) \quad \text{almost surely},$$
and thus
$$M_n = X_n - X_0 - A_n = \sum_{k=1}^{n}(X_k - X_{k-1}) - \sum_{k=1}^{n}\big(\mathbb{E}[X_k \mid \mathcal{F}_{k-1}] - X_{k-1}\big) = \sum_{k=1}^{n}\big(X_k - \mathbb{E}[X_k \mid \mathcal{F}_{k-1}]\big)$$
almost surely. This shows uniqueness.

*(Existence.)* Conversely, set
$$A_n = \sum_{k=1}^{n}\big(\mathbb{E}[X_k \mid \mathcal{F}_{k-1}] - X_{k-1}\big) \quad \text{and} \quad M_n = \sum_{k=1}^{n}\big(X_k - \mathbb{E}[X_k \mid \mathcal{F}_{k-1}]\big)$$
for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$, and let us check that $(A_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ and $(M_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ have the required properties. First, we have
$$A_n + M_n = \sum_{k=1}^{n}(X_k - X_{k-1}) = X_n - X_0,$$
as desired. Moreover, we have $A_0 = 0$ and $M_0 = 0$, and for each $n \in \mathbb{N}^*$, the random variable $A_n$ is $\mathcal{F}_{n-1}$-measurable as a measurable function of $\mathbb{E}[X_1 \mid \mathcal{F}_0], \dots, \mathbb{E}[X_n \mid \mathcal{F}_{n-1}]$ and $X_1, \dots, X_{n-1}$ which are $\mathcal{F}_{n-1}$-measurable. Finally, for each $n \in \mathbb{N}$, the random variable $M_n$ is $\mathcal{F}_n$-measurable and integrable as a linear combination of $X_1, \dots, X_n$ and $\mathbb{E}[X_1 \mid \mathcal{F}_0], \dots, \mathbb{E}[X_n \mid \mathcal{F}_{n-1}]$ which are $\mathcal{F}_n$-measurable and integrable. It remains to check the martingale property: we have
$$\mathbb{E}[M_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = \mathbb{E}\big[M_n + (X_{n+1} - \mathbb{E}[X_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n]) \mid \mathcal{F}_n\big] = M_n + \mathbb{E}[X_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] - \mathbb{E}[X_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = M_n$$
almost surely, which concludes the proof.

**2.** $(X_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ is a submartingale with respect to $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ if and only if for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$, we have $\mathbb{E}[X_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] \ge X_n$ almost surely, if and only if for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$, we have $A_{n+1} - A_n \ge 0$ almost surely.

Similarly, $(X_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ is a supermartingale with respect to $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ if and only if for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$, we have $A_{n+1} \le A_n$ almost surely.

points.label: Unicité : à partir de $X_{n+1}=X_0+A_{n+1}+M_{n+1}$, prendre $\mathbb{E}[\cdot\mid\mathcal{F}_n]$ en utilisant que $A_{n+1}$ est $\mathcal{F}_n$-mesurable (prévisible) et la propriété de martingale ($\mathbb{E}[M_{n+1}\mid\mathcal{F}_n]=M_n$) pour obtenir $A_{n+1}-A_n=\mathbb{E}[X_{n+1}\mid\mathcal{F}_n]-X_n$, d'où $A_n=\sum_{k=1}^n(\mathbb{E}[X_k\mid\mathcal{F}_{k-1}]-X_{k-1})$ et le $M_n$ correspondant.

Existence : poser $A_n=\sum_{k=1}^n(\mathbb{E}[X_k\mid\mathcal{F}_{k-1}]-X_{k-1})$ et $M_n=\sum_{k=1}^n(X_k-\mathbb{E}[X_k\mid\mathcal{F}_{k-1}])$, puis vérifier $A_n+M_n=X_n-X_0$, $A_0=M_0=0$, la prévisibilité de $(A_n)$ ($\mathcal{F}_{n-1}$-mesurable), l'intégrabilité, et la propriété de martingale $\mathbb{E}[M_{n+1}\mid\mathcal{F}_n]=M_n$ p.s.
Partie 2 : sous-martingale $\iff \mathbb{E}[X_{n+1}\mid\mathcal{F}_n]\ge X_n \iff A_{n+1}\ge A_n$ p.s. pour tout $n$ ; énoncé analogue pour une surmartingale : $A_{n+1}\le A_n$ p.s. pour tout $n$.

front_en:
**Exercise 7 (Doob decomposition).** Let $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ be a filtration, let $(X_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ be an adapted process, and assume that $\mathbb{E}[|X_n|] < \infty$ for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$.

1. Prove that there exists a unique decomposition
$$X_n = X_0 + A_n + M_n \quad \text{for all } n \in \mathbb{N},$$
where:
- $A_0 = 0$, and $(A_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}^*}$ is predictable with respect to $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$,
- $M_0 = 0$, and $(M_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ is a martingale with respect to $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$.

2. Prove that $(X_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ is a submartingale with respect to $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ if and only if for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$, we have $A_{n+1} \ge A_n$ almost surely. What is the analogous statement for a supermartingale?
back_en:
**Solution.**

**1.** *(Uniqueness.)* Let $(A_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ and $(M_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ be such a decomposition of $(X_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$. For each $n \in \mathbb{N}$, we have
$$X_{n+1} = X_0 + A_{n+1} + M_{n+1},$$
where $X_{n+1}$, $X_0$ and $M_{n+1}$ are integrable. Thus, the random variable
$$A_{n+1} = X_{n+1} - X_0 - M_{n+1}$$
is integrable, and we can take the conditional expectation given $\mathcal{F}_n$ in $X_{n+1} = X_0 + A_{n+1} + M_{n+1}$.

Since $X_0$ and $A_{n+1}$ are $\mathcal{F}_n$-measurable, and since $(M_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ is a martingale with respect to $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$, we get
$$\mathbb{E}[X_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = X_0 + A_{n+1} + M_n = X_0 + A_{n+1} + (X_n - X_0 - A_n) \quad \text{almost surely},$$
i.e.,
$$A_{n+1} - A_n = \mathbb{E}[X_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] - X_n \quad \text{almost surely}.$$
Since $A_0 = 0$, it follows that
$$A_n = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \big(\mathbb{E}[X_k \mid \mathcal{F}_{k-1}] - X_{k-1}\big) \quad \text{almost surely},$$
and thus
$$M_n = X_n - X_0 - A_n = \sum_{k=1}^{n}(X_k - X_{k-1}) - \sum_{k=1}^{n}\big(\mathbb{E}[X_k \mid \mathcal{F}_{k-1}] - X_{k-1}\big) = \sum_{k=1}^{n}\big(X_k - \mathbb{E}[X_k \mid \mathcal{F}_{k-1}]\big)$$
almost surely. This shows uniqueness.

*(Existence.)* Conversely, set
$$A_n = \sum_{k=1}^{n}\big(\mathbb{E}[X_k \mid \mathcal{F}_{k-1}] - X_{k-1}\big) \quad \text{and} \quad M_n = \sum_{k=1}^{n}\big(X_k - \mathbb{E}[X_k \mid \mathcal{F}_{k-1}]\big)$$
for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$, and let us check that $(A_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ and $(M_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ have the required properties. First, we have
$$A_n + M_n = \sum_{k=1}^{n}(X_k - X_{k-1}) = X_n - X_0,$$
as desired. Moreover, we have $A_0 = 0$ and $M_0 = 0$, and for each $n \in \mathbb{N}^*$, the random variable $A_n$ is $\mathcal{F}_{n-1}$-measurable as a measurable function of $\mathbb{E}[X_1 \mid \mathcal{F}_0], \dots, \mathbb{E}[X_n \mid \mathcal{F}_{n-1}]$ and $X_1, \dots, X_{n-1}$ which are $\mathcal{F}_{n-1}$-measurable. Finally, for each $n \in \mathbb{N}$, the random variable $M_n$ is $\mathcal{F}_n$-measurable and integrable as a linear combination of $X_1, \dots, X_n$ and $\mathbb{E}[X_1 \mid \mathcal{F}_0], \dots, \mathbb{E}[X_n \mid \mathcal{F}_{n-1}]$ which are $\mathcal{F}_n$-measurable and integrable. It remains to check the martingale property: we have
$$\mathbb{E}[M_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = \mathbb{E}\big[M_n + (X_{n+1} - \mathbb{E}[X_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n]) \mid \mathcal{F}_n\big] = M_n + \mathbb{E}[X_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] - \mathbb{E}[X_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = M_n$$
almost surely, which concludes the proof.

**2.** $(X_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ is a submartingale with respect to $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ if and only if for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$, we have $\mathbb{E}[X_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] \ge X_n$ almost surely, if and only if for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$, we have $A_{n+1} - A_n \ge 0$ almost surely.

Similarly, $(X_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ is a supermartingale with respect to $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ if and only if for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$, we have $A_{n+1} \le A_n$ almost surely.

points.label: Uniqueness: from $X_{n+1}=X_0+A_{n+1}+M_{n+1}$, take $\mathbb{E}[\cdot\mid\mathcal{F}_n]$ using that $A_{n+1}$ is $\mathcal{F}_n$-measurable (predictable) and the martingale property ($\mathbb{E}[M_{n+1}\mid\mathcal{F}_n]=M_n$) to obtain $A_{n+1}-A_n=\mathbb{E}[X_{n+1}\mid\mathcal{F}_n]-X_n$, hence $A_n=\sum_{k=1}^n(\mathbb{E}[X_k\mid\mathcal{F}_{k-1}]-X_{k-1})$ and the corresponding $M_n$.

Existence: set $A_n=\sum_{k=1}^n(\mathbb{E}[X_k\mid\mathcal{F}_{k-1}]-X_{k-1})$ and $M_n=\sum_{k=1}^n(X_k-\mathbb{E}[X_k\mid\mathcal{F}_{k-1}])$, then check $A_n+M_n=X_n-X_0$, $A_0=M_0=0$, the predictability of $(A_n)$ ($\mathcal{F}_{n-1}$-measurable), the integrability, and the martingale property $\mathbb{E}[M_{n+1}\mid\mathcal{F}_n]=M_n$ a.s.
Part 2: submartingale $\iff \mathbb{E}[X_{n+1}\mid\mathcal{F}_n]\ge X_n \iff A_{n+1}\ge A_n$ a.s. for all $n$; analogous statement for a supermartingale: $A_{n+1}\le A_n$ a.s. for all $n$.

front:
$\textbf{Exercice 1 (The original martingale).}$ Soit $(X_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}^*}$ une suite de variables aléatoires indépendantes de loi donnée par $\mathbb{P}(X_n = 1) = p \in \,]0,1[\,$ et $\mathbb{P}(X_n = -1) = 1 - p$ pour tout $n \in \mathbb{N}^*$. On pose $S_n = X_1 + \ldots + X_n$ pour tout $n \in \mathbb{N}$, et on note $\mathcal{F}_n = \sigma(X_1, \ldots, X_n)$.

$\textbf{1.}$ Vérifier que $\sigma = \inf\{n \in \mathbb{N} : S_n \ge 1\}$ est un temps d'arrêt. Montrer que si $p \ge 1/2$, alors $\sigma < \infty$ presque sûrement, et si $p < 1/2$, alors $\mathbb{P}(\sigma = \infty) > 0$.

$\textbf{2.}$ Considérer le processus $(H_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}^*}$ donné par $H_1 = 1$, et
$$H_n = \begin{cases} 2H_{n-1} & \text{si } X_{n-1} = -1, \\ 1 & \text{si } X_{n-1} = 1, \end{cases} \qquad \text{pour tout } n \ge 2.$$
Vérifier que pour chaque $n \in \mathbb{N}^*$, la variable aléatoire $H_n$ est $\mathcal{F}_{n-1}$-mesurable, bornée et positive.

$\textbf{3.}$ Vérifier que $\tau = \inf\{n \in \mathbb{N} : (H \cdot S)_n \ge 1\}$ est un temps d'arrêt, et montrer que
$$\tau = \inf\{n \in \mathbb{N}^* : X_n = 1\}.$$
En déduire que $\tau < \infty$ presque sûrement.
back:
$\textbf{1.}$ $\sigma$ est un temps d'arrêt car pour chaque $n \in \mathbb{N}$,
$$(\sigma \le n) = \bigcup_{k=0}^{n} (S_k \ge 1) \in \mathcal{F}_n.$$

$\bullet$ $\textbf{Cas } p \ge 1/2.$ Si $p > 1/2$, par la loi forte des grands nombres $S_n/n \to 2p - 1 > 0$ p.s. lorsque $n \to \infty$, donc $S_n \to \infty$ p.s. ; en particulier $\sigma < \infty$ p.s.

Si $p = 1/2$, la loi forte des grands nombres donne seulement $S_n/n \to 0$ p.s. On montre que $\sup_{n\ge 0} S_n = \infty$ p.s. On a vu (première séance d'exercices) que pour chaque $k \in \mathbb{N}$, $\mathbb{P}(S_n \in [\![-k,k]\!] \text{ pour tout } n \in \mathbb{N}) = 0$. On en déduit
$$\mathbb{P}(\text{la suite } (S_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}} \text{ est bornée}) = \mathbb{P}\Big(\bigcup_{k\ge 0} (S_n \in [\![-k,k]\!] \text{ pour tout } n \in \mathbb{N})\Big) = 0.$$
Avec probabilité $1$, on a donc soit $\sup_{n\ge 0} S_n = \infty$, soit $\inf_{n\ge 0} S_n = -\infty$. Par la borne d'union,
$$1 \le \mathbb{P}\Big(\sup_{n\ge 0} S_n = \infty\Big) + \mathbb{P}\Big(\inf_{n\ge 0} S_n = -\infty\Big).$$
Par symétrie de $S$ (le fait que $(-S_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ a la même loi que $(S_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$), les deux probabilités sont égales, d'où $\mathbb{P}(\sup_{n\ge 0} S_n = \infty) \ge \tfrac{1}{2} > 0$. L'événement $(\sup_{n\ge 0} S_n = \infty)$ appartient à la tribu asymptotique $\mathcal{T} = \bigcap_{n} \sigma(X_k ; k \ge n)$ car pour chaque $n$, $(\sup_{k\ge 0} S_k = \infty) = (\sup_{k\ge n}(X_n + \cdots + X_k) = \infty) \in \sigma(X_k ; k \ge n)$. Par la loi du $0$-$1$ de Kolmogorov, $\mathbb{P}(\sup_{n\ge 0} S_n = \infty) \in \{0,1\}$, donc $\mathbb{P}(\sup_{n\ge 0} S_n = \infty) = 1$, ce qui entraîne $\sigma < \infty$ p.s.

$\bullet$ $\textbf{Cas } p < 1/2.$ Par la loi forte des grands nombres, $S_n/n \to 2p - 1 < 0$ p.s., donc $S_n \to -\infty$ p.s. Ainsi $\sup_{n\ge 0} S_n < \infty$ p.s., et il existe $k \in \mathbb{N}$ tel que $\mathbb{P}(\sup_{n\ge 0} S_n \le k) > 0$. On pose $T_n = \sum_{i=k+1}^{k+n} X_i$ : $(T_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ a la même loi que $(S_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ et est indépendant de $\mathcal{F}_k$. Il s'ensuit
$$\mathbb{P}(\sigma = \infty) \ge \mathbb{P}\Big(X_1 = -1 ; \ldots ; X_k = -1 ; \sup_{n\ge k} T_n \le k\Big) = \mathbb{P}(X_1 = -1 ; \ldots ; X_k = -1)\cdot \mathbb{P}\Big(\sup_{n\ge 0} T_n \le k\Big) = (1-p)^k \cdot \mathbb{P}\Big(\sup_{n\ge 0} S_n \le k\Big) > 0.$$

$\textbf{2.}$ On montre par récurrence sur $k$ que (i) $1 \le H_k \le 2^{k-1}$ et (ii) $H_k$ est $\mathcal{F}_{k-1}$-mesurable. Pour $k = 1$, $H_1 = 1$ donne (i), et (ii) car $\mathcal{F}_0 = \{\emptyset, \Omega\}$. Supposons (i) et (ii) pour $k$ ; comme
$$H_{k+1} = \begin{cases} 2H_k & \text{si } X_k = -1, \\ 1 & \text{si } X_k = 1, \end{cases}$$
on a $1 \le H_{k+1} \le 2^k$ (i)' ; et (ii)' car pour tout $B \in \mathcal{B}(\mathbb{R})$, $(H_{k+1} \in B) = ((2H_k \in B) \cap (X_k = -1)) \sqcup ((1 \in B) \cap (X_k = 1)) \in \mathcal{F}_k$. Donc $H_n$ est $\mathcal{F}_{n-1}$-mesurable, bornée et positive.

$\textbf{3.}$ $\tau$ est un temps d'arrêt comme premier temps d'atteinte du borélien $[1,\infty[ \in \mathcal{B}(\mathbb{R})$ par le processus adapté $((H\cdot S)_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ : pour chaque $n$,
$$(\tau \le n) = \bigcup_{k=0}^{n} ((H\cdot S)_k \ge 1) \in \mathcal{F}_n.$$
On pose $\tau' = \inf\{n \in \mathbb{N}^* : X_n = 1\}$. Pour tout $k \in \mathbb{N}^*$ tel que $k \le \tau'$, on a $H_k = 2^{k-1}$. Donc pour tout $n \in \mathbb{N}$ avec $n < \tau'$,
$$(H\cdot S)_n = \sum_{k=1}^{n} H_k (S_k - S_{k-1}) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} H_k X_k = \sum_{k=1}^{n} 2^{k-1}\cdot(-1) = -(2^n - 1).$$
Si $\tau' = \infty$, alors $(H\cdot S)_n = -(2^n - 1)$ pour tout $n$, et $\tau = \infty$. Si $\tau' < \infty$, alors $(H\cdot S)_n = -(2^n-1) \le 0$ pour tout $n < \tau'$, et
$$(H\cdot S)_{\tau'} = (H\cdot S)_{\tau'-1} + 2^{\tau'-1}\cdot(+1) = -(2^{\tau'-1} - 1) + 2^{\tau'-1} = 1.$$
Dans tous les cas, $\tau' = \tau$, c'est-à-dire $\tau = \inf\{n \in \mathbb{N}^* : X_n = 1\}$. Comme $\mathbb{P}(X_n = 1) = p > 0$ et les $X_n$ sont indépendants, $\tau < \infty$ p.s.

points.label: Partie 1 : justifier que $\sigma$ est un temps d'arret via $(\sigma\le n)=\bigcup_{k=0}^n(S_k\ge 1)\in\mathcal{F}_n$, et traiter $p>1/2$ par la LFGN ($S_n/n\to 2p-1>0$ donc $S_n\to\infty$, $\sigma<\infty$ p.s.).

Partie 1, cas $p=1/2$ : montrer $\sup_{n}S_n=\infty$ p.s. (suite non bornee p.s., symetrie de $S$ donnant $\mathbb{P}(\sup S_n=\infty)\ge 1/2$, puis loi du 0-1 de Kolmogorov sur la tribu asymptotique pour conclure $=1$) ; et cas $p<1/2$ : $S_n\to-\infty$ donc $\exists k$ avec $\mathbb{P}(\sup S_n\le k)>0$, d'ou $\mathbb{P}(\sigma=\infty)\ge(1-p)^k\,\mathbb{P}(\sup S_n\le k)>0$.

Parties 2 et 3 : recurrence montrant $1\le H_n\le 2^{n-1}$ et $H_n$ $\mathcal{F}_{n-1}$-mesurable, bornee, positive ; $\tau$ temps d'arret ; calcul $(H\cdot S)_n=-(2^n-1)$ tant que $n<\tau'$ avec $\tau'=\inf\{n:X_n=1\}$, puis $(H\cdot S)_{\tau'}=1$, d'ou $\tau=\tau'<\infty$ p.s.

front_en:
$\textbf{Exercise 1 (The original martingale).}$ Let $(X_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}^*}$ be a sequence of independent random variables with distribution given by $\mathbb{P}(X_n = 1) = p \in \,]0,1[\,$ and $\mathbb{P}(X_n = -1) = 1 - p$ for all $n \in \mathbb{N}^*$. Set $S_n = X_1 + \ldots + X_n$ for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$, and write $\mathcal{F}_n = \sigma(X_1, \ldots, X_n)$.

$\textbf{1.}$ Verify that $\sigma = \inf\{n \in \mathbb{N} : S_n \ge 1\}$ is a stopping time. Show that if $p \ge 1/2$, then $\sigma < \infty$ almost surely, and if $p < 1/2$, then $\mathbb{P}(\sigma = \infty) > 0$.

$\textbf{2.}$ Consider the process $(H_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}^*}$ given by $H_1 = 1$, and
$$H_n = \begin{cases} 2H_{n-1} & \text{si } X_{n-1} = -1, \\ 1 & \text{si } X_{n-1} = 1, \end{cases} \qquad \text{pour tout } n \ge 2.$$
Verify that for each $n \in \mathbb{N}^*$, the random variable $H_n$ is $\mathcal{F}_{n-1}$-measurable, bounded and nonnegative.

$\textbf{3.}$ Verify that $\tau = \inf\{n \in \mathbb{N} : (H \cdot S)_n \ge 1\}$ is a stopping time, and show that
$$\tau = \inf\{n \in \mathbb{N}^* : X_n = 1\}.$$
Deduce that $\tau < \infty$ almost surely.
back_en:
$\textbf{1.}$ $\sigma$ is a stopping time since for each $n \in \mathbb{N}$,
$$(\sigma \le n) = \bigcup_{k=0}^{n} (S_k \ge 1) \in \mathcal{F}_n.$$

$\bullet$ $\textbf{Case } p \ge 1/2.$ If $p > 1/2$, by the strong law of large numbers $S_n/n \to 2p - 1 > 0$ a.s. as $n \to \infty$, hence $S_n \to \infty$ a.s.; in particular $\sigma < \infty$ a.s.

If $p = 1/2$, the strong law of large numbers only gives $S_n/n \to 0$ a.s. We show that $\sup_{n\ge 0} S_n = \infty$ a.s. We have seen (first exercise session) that for each $k \in \mathbb{N}$, $\mathbb{P}(S_n \in [\![-k,k]\!] \text{ pour tout } n \in \mathbb{N}) = 0$. We deduce
$$\mathbb{P}(\text{la suite } (S_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}} \text{ est bornée}) = \mathbb{P}\Big(\bigcup_{k\ge 0} (S_n \in [\![-k,k]\!] \text{ pour tout } n \in \mathbb{N})\Big) = 0.$$
With probability $1$, we therefore have either $\sup_{n\ge 0} S_n = \infty$, or $\inf_{n\ge 0} S_n = -\infty$. By the union bound,
$$1 \le \mathbb{P}\Big(\sup_{n\ge 0} S_n = \infty\Big) + \mathbb{P}\Big(\inf_{n\ge 0} S_n = -\infty\Big).$$
By symmetry of $S$ (the fact that $(-S_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ has the same distribution as $(S_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$), the two probabilities are equal, hence $\mathbb{P}(\sup_{n\ge 0} S_n = \infty) \ge \tfrac{1}{2} > 0$. The event $(\sup_{n\ge 0} S_n = \infty)$ belongs to the tail $\sigma$-algebra $\mathcal{T} = \bigcap_{n} \sigma(X_k ; k \ge n)$ since for each $n$, $(\sup_{k\ge 0} S_k = \infty) = (\sup_{k\ge n}(X_n + \cdots + X_k) = \infty) \in \sigma(X_k ; k \ge n)$. By Kolmogorov's $0$-$1$ law, $\mathbb{P}(\sup_{n\ge 0} S_n = \infty) \in \{0,1\}$, hence $\mathbb{P}(\sup_{n\ge 0} S_n = \infty) = 1$, which entails $\sigma < \infty$ a.s.

$\bullet$ $\textbf{Case } p < 1/2.$ By the strong law of large numbers, $S_n/n \to 2p - 1 < 0$ a.s., hence $S_n \to -\infty$ a.s. Thus $\sup_{n\ge 0} S_n < \infty$ a.s., and there exists $k \in \mathbb{N}$ such that $\mathbb{P}(\sup_{n\ge 0} S_n \le k) > 0$. Set $T_n = \sum_{i=k+1}^{k+n} X_i$: $(T_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ has the same distribution as $(S_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ and is independent of $\mathcal{F}_k$. It follows that
$$\mathbb{P}(\sigma = \infty) \ge \mathbb{P}\Big(X_1 = -1 ; \ldots ; X_k = -1 ; \sup_{n\ge k} T_n \le k\Big) = \mathbb{P}(X_1 = -1 ; \ldots ; X_k = -1)\cdot \mathbb{P}\Big(\sup_{n\ge 0} T_n \le k\Big) = (1-p)^k \cdot \mathbb{P}\Big(\sup_{n\ge 0} S_n \le k\Big) > 0.$$

$\textbf{2.}$ We show by induction on $k$ that (i) $1 \le H_k \le 2^{k-1}$ and (ii) $H_k$ is $\mathcal{F}_{k-1}$-measurable. For $k = 1$, $H_1 = 1$ gives (i), and (ii) since $\mathcal{F}_0 = \{\emptyset, \Omega\}$. Assume (i) and (ii) for $k$; since
$$H_{k+1} = \begin{cases} 2H_k & \text{si } X_k = -1, \\ 1 & \text{si } X_k = 1, \end{cases}$$
we have $1 \le H_{k+1} \le 2^k$ (i)'; and (ii)' since for all $B \in \mathcal{B}(\mathbb{R})$, $(H_{k+1} \in B) = ((2H_k \in B) \cap (X_k = -1)) \sqcup ((1 \in B) \cap (X_k = 1)) \in \mathcal{F}_k$. Hence $H_n$ is $\mathcal{F}_{n-1}$-measurable, bounded and nonnegative.

$\textbf{3.}$ $\tau$ is a stopping time as the first hitting time of the Borel set $[1,\infty[ \in \mathcal{B}(\mathbb{R})$ by the adapted process $((H\cdot S)_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$: for each $n$,
$$(\tau \le n) = \bigcup_{k=0}^{n} ((H\cdot S)_k \ge 1) \in \mathcal{F}_n.$$
Set $\tau' = \inf\{n \in \mathbb{N}^* : X_n = 1\}$. For all $k \in \mathbb{N}^*$ such that $k \le \tau'$, we have $H_k = 2^{k-1}$. Hence for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$ with $n < \tau'$,
$$(H\cdot S)_n = \sum_{k=1}^{n} H_k (S_k - S_{k-1}) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} H_k X_k = \sum_{k=1}^{n} 2^{k-1}\cdot(-1) = -(2^n - 1).$$
If $\tau' = \infty$, then $(H\cdot S)_n = -(2^n - 1)$ for all $n$, and $\tau = \infty$. If $\tau' < \infty$, then $(H\cdot S)_n = -(2^n-1) \le 0$ for all $n < \tau'$, and
$$(H\cdot S)_{\tau'} = (H\cdot S)_{\tau'-1} + 2^{\tau'-1}\cdot(+1) = -(2^{\tau'-1} - 1) + 2^{\tau'-1} = 1.$$
In all cases, $\tau' = \tau$, that is $\tau = \inf\{n \in \mathbb{N}^* : X_n = 1\}$. Since $\mathbb{P}(X_n = 1) = p > 0$ and the $X_n$ are independent, $\tau < \infty$ a.s.

points.label: Part 1: justify that $\sigma$ is a stopping time via $(\sigma\le n)=\bigcup_{k=0}^n(S_k\ge 1)\in\mathcal{F}_n$, and handle $p>1/2$ by the SLLN ($S_n/n\to 2p-1>0$ so $S_n\to\infty$, $\sigma<\infty$ a.s.).

Part 1, case $p=1/2$: show $\sup_{n}S_n=\infty$ a.s. (sequence unbounded a.s., symmetry of $S$ giving $\mathbb{P}(\sup S_n=\infty)\ge 1/2$, then Kolmogorov's 0-1 law on the tail $\sigma$-algebra to conclude $=1$); and case $p<1/2$: $S_n\to-\infty$ so $\exists k$ with $\mathbb{P}(\sup S_n\le k)>0$, hence $\mathbb{P}(\sigma=\infty)\ge(1-p)^k\,\mathbb{P}(\sup S_n\le k)>0$.

Parts 2 and 3: induction showing $1\le H_n\le 2^{n-1}$ and $H_n$ $\mathcal{F}_{n-1}$-measurable, bounded, nonnegative; $\tau$ a stopping time; computation $(H\cdot S)_n=-(2^n-1)$ as long as $n<\tau'$ with $\tau'=\inf\{n:X_n=1\}$, then $(H\cdot S)_{\tau'}=1$, hence $\tau=\tau'<\infty$ a.s.

front: **Exercice 2.** Montrer que $\tau : \Omega \to [\![0,\infty]\!]$ est un temps d'arrêt par rapport à $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ si et seulement si pour tout $n \in \mathbb{N}$, on a $(\tau = n) \in \mathcal{F}_n$.
back:
**Solution.** Si $(\tau \le n) \in \mathcal{F}_n$ pour tout $n \in \mathbb{N}$, alors
$$(\tau = n) = (\tau \le n) \setminus (\tau \le n-1) \in \mathcal{F}_n.$$
Réciproquement, si $(\tau = n) \in \mathcal{F}_n$ pour tout $n \in \mathbb{N}$, alors
$$(\tau \le n) = \bigcup_{k=0}^{n} (\tau = k) \in \mathcal{F}_n.$$
$\square$

points.label: Sens direct : à partir de $(\tau \le n) \in \mathcal{F}_n$, écrire $(\tau = n) = (\tau \le n) \setminus (\tau \le n-1)$ et conclure $(\tau = n) \in \mathcal{F}_n$
Sens réciproque : à partir de $(\tau = k) \in \mathcal{F}_k \subseteq \mathcal{F}_n$ (pour $k \le n$), écrire $(\tau \le n) = \bigcup_{k=0}^{n} (\tau = k)$
Conclure que cette union finie d'éléments de $\mathcal{F}_n$ appartient à $\mathcal{F}_n$, donc $(\tau \le n) \in \mathcal{F}_n$

front_en: **Exercise 2.** Show that $\tau : \Omega \to [\![0,\infty]\!]$ is a stopping time with respect to $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ if and only if for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$, we have $(\tau = n) \in \mathcal{F}_n$.
back_en:
**Solution.** If $(\tau \le n) \in \mathcal{F}_n$ for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$, then
$$(\tau = n) = (\tau \le n) \setminus (\tau \le n-1) \in \mathcal{F}_n.$$
Conversely, if $(\tau = n) \in \mathcal{F}_n$ for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$, then
$$(\tau \le n) = \bigcup_{k=0}^{n} (\tau = k) \in \mathcal{F}_n.$$
$\square$

points.label: Forward direction: from $(\tau \le n) \in \mathcal{F}_n$, write $(\tau = n) = (\tau \le n) \setminus (\tau \le n-1)$ and conclude $(\tau = n) \in \mathcal{F}_n$
Converse direction: from $(\tau = k) \in \mathcal{F}_k \subseteq \mathcal{F}_n$ (for $k \le n$), write $(\tau \le n) = \bigcup_{k=0}^{n} (\tau = k)$
Conclude that this finite union of elements of $\mathcal{F}_n$ belongs to $\mathcal{F}_n$, hence $(\tau \le n) \in \mathcal{F}_n$

front:
**Exercice 3.** Soit $(X_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}^*}$ une suite de variables aléatoires indépendantes de loi donnée par $\mathbb{P}(X_n = 1) = 1/2 = \mathbb{P}(X_n = -1)$ pour tout $n \in \mathbb{N}^*$. On pose $S_n = X_1 + \ldots + X_n$ pour tout $n \in \mathbb{N}$, et soit $\mathcal{F}_n = \sigma(X_1, \ldots, X_n)$. Fixons $n \in \mathbb{N}^*$, et posons
$$\tau = \max\{k \in [\![0, 2n]\!] : S_k = 0\}.$$
Montrer que $\tau$ n'est pas un temps d'arrêt par rapport à $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$.
back:
**Solution.** Par la définition de $\tau$, on a
$$0 = \mathbb{P}(\tau = 2n - 2 \,;\, X_{2n-1} = 1 \,;\, X_{2n} = -1)$$
$$\neq \mathbb{P}(\tau = 2n - 2) \cdot \mathbb{P}(X_{2n-1} = 1 \,;\, X_{2n} = -1) > 0.$$

En effet, on a
$$\mathbb{P}(\tau = 2n - 2) \ge \mathbb{P}(X_1 = 1 \,;\, \ldots \,;\, X_{n-1} = 1 \,;\, X_n = -1 \,;\, \ldots \,;\, X_{2n-2} = -1) > 0.$$

(L'égalité de gauche $0 = \mathbb{P}(\tau = 2n-2 \,;\, X_{2n-1}=1 \,;\, X_{2n}=-1)$ vient du fait que si $S_{2n-2} = 0$ — ce qui est imposé par $\tau = 2n-2$ — et $X_{2n-1} = 1$, $X_{2n} = -1$, alors $S_{2n} = 0$, donc $\tau \ge 2n > 2n-2$ : l'événement $\{\tau = 2n-2 \,;\, X_{2n-1}=1 \,;\, X_{2n}=-1\}$ est vide, de probabilité $0$.)

Cependant, pour tout événement $A \in \mathcal{F}_{2n-2}$, comme $(X_{2n-1}, X_{2n})$ est indépendant de $\mathcal{F}_{2n-2}$, on a
$$\mathbb{P}(A \,;\, X_{2n-1} = 1 \,;\, X_{2n} = -1) = \mathbb{P}(A) \cdot \mathbb{P}(X_{2n-1} = 1 \,;\, X_{2n} = -1).$$

Ceci montre que $\{\tau = 2n - 2\} \notin \mathcal{F}_{2n-2}$. Comme $\{\tau = 2n-2\}$ n'appartient pas à $\mathcal{F}_{2n-2}$, $\tau$ n'est pas un temps d'arrêt par rapport à $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$. $\square$

points.label: Identifier que $\{\tau = 2n-2\,;\,X_{2n-1}=1\,;\,X_{2n}=-1\}$ est de probabilité nulle (événement impossible : $S_{2n-2}=0$ avec $X_{2n-1}=1,\,X_{2n}=-1$ donne $S_{2n}=0$, donc $\tau\ge 2n$).

Montrer que $\mathbb{P}(\tau = 2n-2) > 0$ via une trajectoire explicite (ex. $X_1=\cdots=X_{n-1}=1$, $X_n=\cdots=X_{2n-2}=-1$), donc $\mathbb{P}(\tau=2n-2)\cdot\mathbb{P}(X_{2n-1}=1\,;\,X_{2n}=-1) > 0$.

Conclure par contradiction : si $A=\{\tau=2n-2\}\in\mathcal{F}_{2n-2}$, l'indépendance de $(X_{2n-1},X_{2n})$ avec $\mathcal{F}_{2n-2}$ donnerait $\mathbb{P}(A\,;\,X_{2n-1}=1\,;\,X_{2n}=-1)=\mathbb{P}(A)\cdot\mathbb{P}(\ldots)>0$, contredisant la valeur $0$ ; donc $\{\tau=2n-2\}\notin\mathcal{F}_{2n-2}$ et $\tau$ n'est pas un temps d'arrêt.

front_en:
**Exercise 3.** Let $(X_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}^*}$ be a sequence of independent random variables with distribution given by $\mathbb{P}(X_n = 1) = 1/2 = \mathbb{P}(X_n = -1)$ for all $n \in \mathbb{N}^*$. Set $S_n = X_1 + \ldots + X_n$ for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$, and let $\mathcal{F}_n = \sigma(X_1, \ldots, X_n)$. Fix $n \in \mathbb{N}^*$, and set
$$\tau = \max\{k \in [\![0, 2n]\!] : S_k = 0\}.$$
Show that $\tau$ is not a stopping time with respect to $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$.
back_en:
**Solution.** By the definition of $\tau$, we have
$$0 = \mathbb{P}(\tau = 2n - 2 \,;\, X_{2n-1} = 1 \,;\, X_{2n} = -1)$$
$$\neq \mathbb{P}(\tau = 2n - 2) \cdot \mathbb{P}(X_{2n-1} = 1 \,;\, X_{2n} = -1) > 0.$$

Indeed, we have
$$\mathbb{P}(\tau = 2n - 2) \ge \mathbb{P}(X_1 = 1 \,;\, \ldots \,;\, X_{n-1} = 1 \,;\, X_n = -1 \,;\, \ldots \,;\, X_{2n-2} = -1) > 0.$$

(The left-hand equality $0 = \mathbb{P}(\tau = 2n-2 \,;\, X_{2n-1}=1 \,;\, X_{2n}=-1)$ comes from the fact that if $S_{2n-2} = 0$ — which is imposed by $\tau = 2n-2$ — and $X_{2n-1} = 1$, $X_{2n} = -1$, then $S_{2n} = 0$, hence $\tau \ge 2n > 2n-2$: the event $\{\tau = 2n-2 \,;\, X_{2n-1}=1 \,;\, X_{2n}=-1\}$ is empty, of probability $0$.)

However, for every event $A \in \mathcal{F}_{2n-2}$, since $(X_{2n-1}, X_{2n})$ is independent of $\mathcal{F}_{2n-2}$, we have
$$\mathbb{P}(A \,;\, X_{2n-1} = 1 \,;\, X_{2n} = -1) = \mathbb{P}(A) \cdot \mathbb{P}(X_{2n-1} = 1 \,;\, X_{2n} = -1).$$

This shows that $\{\tau = 2n - 2\} \notin \mathcal{F}_{2n-2}$. Since $\{\tau = 2n-2\}$ does not belong to $\mathcal{F}_{2n-2}$, $\tau$ is not a stopping time with respect to $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$. $\square$

points.label: Identify that $\{\tau = 2n-2\,;\,X_{2n-1}=1\,;\,X_{2n}=-1\}$ has probability zero (impossible event: $S_{2n-2}=0$ with $X_{2n-1}=1,\,X_{2n}=-1$ gives $S_{2n}=0$, hence $\tau\ge 2n$).
Show that $\mathbb{P}(\tau = 2n-2) > 0$ via an explicit trajectory (e.g. $X_1=\cdots=X_{n-1}=1$, $X_n=\cdots=X_{2n-2}=-1$), hence $\mathbb{P}(\tau=2n-2)\cdot\mathbb{P}(X_{2n-1}=1\,;\,X_{2n}=-1) > 0$.

Conclude by contradiction: if $A=\{\tau=2n-2\}\in\mathcal{F}_{2n-2}$, the independence of $(X_{2n-1},X_{2n})$ from $\mathcal{F}_{2n-2}$ would give $\mathbb{P}(A\,;\,X_{2n-1}=1\,;\,X_{2n}=-1)=\mathbb{P}(A)\cdot\mathbb{P}(\ldots)>0$, contradicting the value $0$; hence $\{\tau=2n-2\}\notin\mathcal{F}_{2n-2}$ and $\tau$ is not a stopping time.

front:
**Exercice 4.** Soit $\tau$ un temps d'arrêt par rapport à $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$, et soit
$$\mathcal{F}_\tau = \{A \in \mathcal{F}_\infty : A \cap (\tau \le n) \in \mathcal{F}_n \text{ pour tout } n \in \mathbb{N}\}.$$
Vérifier que $\mathcal{F}_\tau$ est une $\sigma$-algèbre, et démontrer que
$$\mathcal{F}_\tau = \{A \in \mathcal{F}_\infty : A \cap (\tau = n) \in \mathcal{F}_n \text{ pour tout } n \in \mathbb{N}\}.$$
back:
**Solution.**

**$\mathcal{F}_\tau$ est une $\sigma$-algèbre :**

- $\emptyset \in \mathcal{F}_\infty$ et $\emptyset \cap (\tau \le n) = \emptyset \in \mathcal{F}_n$ pour tout $n \in \mathbb{N}$, donc $\emptyset \in \mathcal{F}_\tau$.

- Si $A \in \mathcal{F}_\tau$, alors $\Omega \setminus A \in \mathcal{F}_\infty$, et pour tout $n \in \mathbb{N}$ on a
$$(\Omega \setminus A) \cap (\tau \le n) = (\tau \le n) \setminus (A \cap (\tau \le n)) \in \mathcal{F}_n,$$
donc $\Omega \setminus A \in \mathcal{F}_\tau$.

- Si $A_1, A_2, \ldots \in \mathcal{F}_\tau$, alors $\bigcup_{k\ge 1} A_k \in \mathcal{F}_\infty$, et pour tout $n \in \mathbb{N}$ on a
$$\left(\bigcup_{k\ge 1} A_k\right) \cap (\tau \le n) = \bigcup_{k\ge 1}\left(A_k \cap (\tau \le n)\right) \in \mathcal{F}_n,$$
donc $\bigcup_{k\ge 1} A_k \in \mathcal{F}_\tau$.

Ceci montre que $\mathcal{F}_\tau$ est une $\sigma$-algèbre.

**Égalité des deux descriptions.** Montrons finalement que
$$\mathcal{F}_\tau = \{A \in \mathcal{F}_\infty : A \cap (\tau \le n) \in \mathcal{F}_n \text{ pour tout } n \in \mathbb{N}\}.$$
En fait, pour chaque $A \in \mathcal{F}_\infty$, ce qui suit est vrai :

- Si $A \cap (\tau \le n) \in \mathcal{F}_n$ pour tout $n \in \mathbb{N}$, alors
$$A \cap (\tau = n) = (A \cap (\tau \le n)) \setminus (A \cap (\tau \le n-1)) \in \mathcal{F}_n.$$

- Si $A \cap (\tau = n) \in \mathcal{F}_n$ pour tout $n \in \mathbb{N}$, alors
$$A \cap (\tau \le n) = \bigcup_{k=0}^{n} (A \cap (\tau = k)) \in \mathcal{F}_n.$$

Ces deux implications montrent que les deux ensembles définis par les conditions $A \cap (\tau \le n) \in \mathcal{F}_n$ et $A \cap (\tau = n) \in \mathcal{F}_n$ coïncident, d'où l'égalité annoncée.

points.label: Stabilité par complément : $\Omega\setminus A \in \mathcal{F}_\infty$ et $(\Omega\setminus A)\cap(\tau\le n) = (\tau\le n)\setminus(A\cap(\tau\le n)) \in \mathcal{F}_n$ (avec $\emptyset \in \mathcal{F}_\tau$), justifiant la propriété de complémentation

Stabilité par réunion dénombrable : $\left(\bigcup_{k\ge1} A_k\right)\cap(\tau\le n) = \bigcup_{k\ge1}(A_k\cap(\tau\le n)) \in \mathcal{F}_n$, concluant que $\mathcal{F}_\tau$ est une $\sigma$-algèbre
Équivalence des deux conditions via $A\cap(\tau=n) = (A\cap(\tau\le n))\setminus(A\cap(\tau\le n-1))$ et $A\cap(\tau\le n) = \bigcup_{k=0}^{n}(A\cap(\tau=k))$, toutes deux dans $\mathcal{F}_n$

front_en:
**Exercise 4.** Let $\tau$ be a stopping time with respect to $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$, and let
$$\mathcal{F}_\tau = \{A \in \mathcal{F}_\infty : A \cap (\tau \le n) \in \mathcal{F}_n \text{ pour tout } n \in \mathbb{N}\}.$$
Verify that $\mathcal{F}_\tau$ is a $\sigma$-algebra, and prove that
$$\mathcal{F}_\tau = \{A \in \mathcal{F}_\infty : A \cap (\tau = n) \in \mathcal{F}_n \text{ pour tout } n \in \mathbb{N}\}.$$
back_en:
**Solution.**

**$\mathcal{F}_\tau$ is a $\sigma$-algebra:**

- $\emptyset \in \mathcal{F}_\infty$ and $\emptyset \cap (\tau \le n) = \emptyset \in \mathcal{F}_n$ for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$, hence $\emptyset \in \mathcal{F}_\tau$.

- If $A \in \mathcal{F}_\tau$, then $\Omega \setminus A \in \mathcal{F}_\infty$, and for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$ we have
$$(\Omega \setminus A) \cap (\tau \le n) = (\tau \le n) \setminus (A \cap (\tau \le n)) \in \mathcal{F}_n,$$
hence $\Omega \setminus A \in \mathcal{F}_\tau$.

- If $A_1, A_2, \ldots \in \mathcal{F}_\tau$, then $\bigcup_{k\ge 1} A_k \in \mathcal{F}_\infty$, and for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$ we have
$$\left(\bigcup_{k\ge 1} A_k\right) \cap (\tau \le n) = \bigcup_{k\ge 1}\left(A_k \cap (\tau \le n)\right) \in \mathcal{F}_n,$$
hence $\bigcup_{k\ge 1} A_k \in \mathcal{F}_\tau$.

This shows that $\mathcal{F}_\tau$ is a $\sigma$-algebra.

**Equality of the two descriptions.** Let us finally show that
$$\mathcal{F}_\tau = \{A \in \mathcal{F}_\infty : A \cap (\tau \le n) \in \mathcal{F}_n \text{ pour tout } n \in \mathbb{N}\}.$$
In fact, for each $A \in \mathcal{F}_\infty$, the following holds:

- If $A \cap (\tau \le n) \in \mathcal{F}_n$ for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$, then
$$A \cap (\tau = n) = (A \cap (\tau \le n)) \setminus (A \cap (\tau \le n-1)) \in \mathcal{F}_n.$$

- If $A \cap (\tau = n) \in \mathcal{F}_n$ for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$, then
$$A \cap (\tau \le n) = \bigcup_{k=0}^{n} (A \cap (\tau = k)) \in \mathcal{F}_n.$$

These two implications show that the two sets defined by the conditions $A \cap (\tau \le n) \in \mathcal{F}_n$ and $A \cap (\tau = n) \in \mathcal{F}_n$ coincide, whence the announced equality.

points.label: Stability under complement: $\Omega\setminus A \in \mathcal{F}_\infty$ and $(\Omega\setminus A)\cap(\tau\le n) = (\tau\le n)\setminus(A\cap(\tau\le n)) \in \mathcal{F}_n$ (with $\emptyset \in \mathcal{F}_\tau$), justifying the complementation property
Stability under countable union: $\left(\bigcup_{k\ge1} A_k\right)\cap(\tau\le n) = \bigcup_{k\ge1}(A_k\cap(\tau\le n)) \in \mathcal{F}_n$, concluding that $\mathcal{F}_\tau$ is a $\sigma$-algebra
Equivalence of the two conditions via $A\cap(\tau=n) = (A\cap(\tau\le n))\setminus(A\cap(\tau\le n-1))$ and $A\cap(\tau\le n) = \bigcup_{k=0}^{n}(A\cap(\tau=k))$, both in $\mathcal{F}_n$

front:
**Exercice 5.** Soient $\sigma$ et $\tau$ deux temps d'arrêt par rapport à $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$.

1. Montrer que si $\sigma \le \tau$, alors $\mathcal{F}_\sigma \subset \mathcal{F}_\tau$.

2. Montrer que $\sigma \wedge \tau$ est un temps d'arrêt par rapport à $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$, et que $\mathcal{F}_{\sigma\wedge\tau} = \mathcal{F}_\sigma \cap \mathcal{F}_\tau$.
back:
**1.** Fixons $A \in \mathcal{F}_\sigma$, et montrons que $A \in \mathcal{F}_\tau$. D'abord, on a $A \in \mathcal{F}_\infty$. Puis, pour tout $n \in \mathbb{N}$, on a

$$A \cap (\tau = n) = \bigsqcup_{k=0}^{n} A \cap (\tau = n) \cap (\sigma = k).$$

Puisque $A \in \mathcal{F}_\sigma$, on a $A \cap (\sigma = k) \in \mathcal{F}_k \subset \mathcal{F}_n$ pour tout $k \in [\![0, n]\!]$, d'où

$$A \cap (\tau = n) = \left( \bigcup_{k=0}^{n} A \cap (\sigma = k) \right) \cap (\tau = n) \in \mathcal{F}_n,$$

comme voulu.

**2.** Pour tout $n \in \mathbb{N}$, on a $(\sigma \wedge \tau \le n) = (\sigma \le n) \cup (\tau \le n) \in \mathcal{F}_n$, donc $\sigma \wedge \tau$ est un temps d'arrêt. Il découle du point précédent que $\mathcal{F}_{\sigma\wedge\tau} \subset \mathcal{F}_\sigma \cap \mathcal{F}_\tau$. Réciproquement, si $A \in \mathcal{F}_\sigma \cap \mathcal{F}_\tau$, alors $A \in \mathcal{F}_\infty$, et pour tout $n \in \mathbb{N}$, on a

$$A \cap (\sigma \wedge \tau \le n) = A \cap \big((\sigma \le n) \cup (\tau \le n)\big) = \big(A \cap (\sigma \le n)\big) \cup \big(A \cap (\tau \le n)\big) \in \mathcal{F}_n,$$

ce qui montre que $A \in \mathcal{F}_{\sigma\wedge\tau}$.

points.label: Point 1 : pour $A \in \mathcal{F}_\sigma$ et $n \in \mathbb{N}$, décomposer $A \cap (\tau = n) = \bigsqcup_{k=0}^{n} A \cap (\tau = n) \cap (\sigma = k)$, utiliser $A \cap (\sigma = k) \in \mathcal{F}_k \subset \mathcal{F}_n$ pour $k \le n$, et conclure $A \cap (\tau = n) \in \mathcal{F}_n$, donc $A \in \mathcal{F}_\tau$.
Point 2 (temps d'arrêt) : montrer que pour tout $n$, $(\sigma \wedge \tau \le n) = (\sigma \le n) \cup (\tau \le n) \in \mathcal{F}_n$, donc $\sigma \wedge \tau$ est un temps d'arrêt.

Point 2 (égalité des tribus) : inclusion $\mathcal{F}_{\sigma\wedge\tau} \subset \mathcal{F}_\sigma \cap \mathcal{F}_\tau$ via le point 1 (car $\sigma\wedge\tau \le \sigma$ et $\le \tau$), puis réciproque : pour $A \in \mathcal{F}_\sigma \cap \mathcal{F}_\tau$, $A \cap (\sigma\wedge\tau \le n) = (A \cap (\sigma \le n)) \cup (A \cap (\tau \le n)) \in \mathcal{F}_n$, d'où $A \in \mathcal{F}_{\sigma\wedge\tau}$.

front_en:
**Exercise 5.** Let $\sigma$ and $\tau$ be two stopping times with respect to $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$.

1. Show that if $\sigma \le \tau$, then $\mathcal{F}_\sigma \subset \mathcal{F}_\tau$.

2. Show that $\sigma \wedge \tau$ is a stopping time with respect to $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$, and that $\mathcal{F}_{\sigma\wedge\tau} = \mathcal{F}_\sigma \cap \mathcal{F}_\tau$.
back_en:
**1.** Fix $A \in \mathcal{F}_\sigma$, and let us show that $A \in \mathcal{F}_\tau$. First, we have $A \in \mathcal{F}_\infty$. Then, for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$, we have

$$A \cap (\tau = n) = \bigsqcup_{k=0}^{n} A \cap (\tau = n) \cap (\sigma = k).$$

Since $A \in \mathcal{F}_\sigma$, we have $A \cap (\sigma = k) \in \mathcal{F}_k \subset \mathcal{F}_n$ for all $k \in [\![0, n]\!]$, whence

$$A \cap (\tau = n) = \left( \bigcup_{k=0}^{n} A \cap (\sigma = k) \right) \cap (\tau = n) \in \mathcal{F}_n,$$

as desired.

**2.** For all $n \in \mathbb{N}$, we have $(\sigma \wedge \tau \le n) = (\sigma \le n) \cup (\tau \le n) \in \mathcal{F}_n$, hence $\sigma \wedge \tau$ is a stopping time. It follows from the previous point that $\mathcal{F}_{\sigma\wedge\tau} \subset \mathcal{F}_\sigma \cap \mathcal{F}_\tau$. Conversely, if $A \in \mathcal{F}_\sigma \cap \mathcal{F}_\tau$, then $A \in \mathcal{F}_\infty$, and for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$, we have

$$A \cap (\sigma \wedge \tau \le n) = A \cap \big((\sigma \le n) \cup (\tau \le n)\big) = \big(A \cap (\sigma \le n)\big) \cup \big(A \cap (\tau \le n)\big) \in \mathcal{F}_n,$$

which shows that $A \in \mathcal{F}_{\sigma\wedge\tau}$.

points.label: Point 1: for $A \in \mathcal{F}_\sigma$ and $n \in \mathbb{N}$, decompose $A \cap (\tau = n) = \bigsqcup_{k=0}^{n} A \cap (\tau = n) \cap (\sigma = k)$, use $A \cap (\sigma = k) \in \mathcal{F}_k \subset \mathcal{F}_n$ for $k \le n$, and conclude $A \cap (\tau = n) \in \mathcal{F}_n$, hence $A \in \mathcal{F}_\tau$.
Point 2 (stopping time): show that for all $n$, $(\sigma \wedge \tau \le n) = (\sigma \le n) \cup (\tau \le n) \in \mathcal{F}_n$, hence $\sigma \wedge \tau$ is a stopping time.

Point 2 (equality of $\sigma$-algebras): inclusion $\mathcal{F}_{\sigma\wedge\tau} \subset \mathcal{F}_\sigma \cap \mathcal{F}_\tau$ via point 1 (since $\sigma\wedge\tau \le \sigma$ and $\le \tau$), then converse: for $A \in \mathcal{F}_\sigma \cap \mathcal{F}_\tau$, $A \cap (\sigma\wedge\tau \le n) = (A \cap (\sigma \le n)) \cup (A \cap (\tau \le n)) \in \mathcal{F}_n$, hence $A \in \mathcal{F}_{\sigma\wedge\tau}$.

front:
**Exercice 6** (Wald's identity). Soit $(X_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}^*}$ une suite de variables aléatoires indépendantes et identiquement distribuées, et supposons que $\mathbb{E}[|X_1|] < \infty$. Posons $S_n = X_1 + \ldots + X_n$ pour tout $n \in \mathbb{N}$, et soit $\mathcal{F}_n = \sigma(X_1, \ldots, X_n)$. De plus, soit $\tau$ un temps d'arrêt par rapport à $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$, et supposons que $\mathbb{E}[\tau] < \infty$. Montrer que $S_\tau$ est intégrable et que
$$\mathbb{E}[S_\tau] = \mathbb{E}[X_1] \cdot \mathbb{E}[\tau].$$
back:
**Solution.** Rappelons que $(M_n = S_n - \mathbb{E}[X_1] \cdot n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ est une martingale par rapport à $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$. Par le théorème d'arrêt optionnel, le processus arrêté $(M_{n \wedge \tau})_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ est une martingale par rapport à $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$. En particulier, on a, pour tout $n \in \mathbb{N}$,
$$\mathbb{E}[S_{n \wedge \tau} - \mathbb{E}[X_1] \cdot (\tau \wedge n)] = \mathbb{E}[M_{n \wedge \tau}] = \mathbb{E}[M_{0 \wedge \tau}] = 0.$$

Comme $S_{n \wedge \tau}$ et $\tau \wedge n$ sont intégrables ($\tau \wedge n$ est borné, et $|S_{n \wedge \tau}| \le \sum_{i=1}^{n \wedge \tau} |X_i| \le \sum_{i=1}^n |X_i|$), on peut réarranger comme suit :
$$\mathbb{E}[S_{\tau \wedge n}] = \mathbb{E}[X_1] \cdot \mathbb{E}[\tau \wedge n].$$

Maintenant, on veut laisser $n \to \infty$. D'une part, on a $\mathbb{E}[\tau \wedge n] \uparrow \mathbb{E}[\tau]$ lorsque $n \uparrow \infty$ par le théorème de convergence monotone. D'autre part, comme $\tau$ est intégrable, on a $\tau < \infty$ presque sûrement, et il s'ensuit que $S_{\tau \wedge n} \to S_\tau$ presque sûrement lorsque $n \to \infty$. Pour conclure que $S_\tau$ est intégrable et que $\mathbb{E}[S_{\tau \wedge n}] \to \mathbb{E}[S_\tau]$ lorsque $n \to \infty$, par le théorème de convergence dominée, il suffit de montrer que $\sum_{i=1}^\tau |X_i|$ est intégrable. En effet, on a
$$|S_{\tau \wedge n}| \le \sum_{i=1}^{\tau \wedge n} |X_i| \le \sum_{i=1}^\tau |X_i| \quad \text{pour tout } n \in \mathbb{N}.$$

Maintenant, on a le même problème qu'auparavant : on veut calculer l'espérance de $\sum_{i=1}^\tau |X_i|$, où $\tau$ est le même temps d'arrêt intégrable. Sauf que maintenant les sommands sont non négatifs ! Suivons la même stratégie. On argumente que $(N_n = \sum_{i=1}^n |X_i| - \mathbb{E}[|X_1|] \cdot n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ est une martingale par rapport à $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$. Par le théorème d'arrêt optionnel, le processus arrêté $(N_{n \wedge \tau})_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ est une martingale par rapport à $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$. En particulier, on a, pour tout $n \in \mathbb{N}$,
$$\mathbb{E}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{\tau \wedge n} |X_i| - \mathbb{E}[|X_1|] \cdot (\tau \wedge n)\right] = \mathbb{E}[N_{n \wedge \tau}] = \mathbb{E}[N_{0 \wedge \tau}] = 0.$$
